reports.title: Hotel switch from IGI airport
reports.desc: Driver claims hotel closed/full, takes you to commission hotel paying him 30–50%.

reports.title: Fake 'Government Tourist Office' in CP/Paharganj
reports.desc: Sells fake train tickets, overpriced tours. Real one is at 88 Janpath Road.

reports.title: New Delhi Railway Station 'tourist office' upstairs
reports.desc: Real IRCTC International Tourist Bureau is on 1st floor. Touts redirect you to fake one.

reports.title: Fake police in Paharganj
reports.desc: Plainclothes 'officers' demand passport check + on-spot fine. Real police don't fine on the spot.

reports.title: Auto refusing meter / inflated fares
reports.desc: Use Uber/Ola — they're 30–60% cheaper than tourist auto rates.

reports.title: Pickpocketing in Chandni Chowk metro
reports.desc: Yellow line during rush hour is hotspot. Front pocket only, zipped bag.

reports.title: Bird poop / shirt stain distraction in CP
reports.desc: Two-person team. Step back, don't let anyone touch you.

reports.title: Hotel Switch / 'Your hotel burned down'
reports.desc: Taxi/auto driver claims your booked hotel is closed, burned, overbooked, or in a 'riot zone'. Drives you to a partner hotel paying 30–50% commission. Currently most active at IGI Airport T3 (after midnight arrivals) and New Delhi Railway Station.

reports.title: Sakha — women-only taxis run by women
reports.desc: Social enterprise; all-women drivers from marginalised communities. >1M safe rides reported. ₹200–600 city rides. Book via app or call.

reports.title: Delhi Metro is genuinely safe for solo women
reports.desc: Women-only first coach. CCTV on all platforms. Late-night service 11pm. Avoid sketchy autos by taking metro to closest station.

reports.title: Fake 'invalid ticket' man at New Delhi Railway Station
reports.place: New Delhi Railway Station, main entrance
reports.desc: Man approaches solo travellers (especially women) claiming their IRCTC ticket is invalid and redirects them to a fake 'official' office nearby. Influencer Klaudia Falenta documented this exact scam in Dec 2025. Your ticket is valid — ignore anyone who says otherwise. Go straight to the platform.

reports.title: Fake 'Tourist Information Office' at 11A Connaught Circle
reports.desc: Scammers at NDLS ticket counters redirect tourists to this fake 'Tourist Information & Reservation Office'. Inside, men posing as officers show fake images claiming trains are cancelled, then quote ₹17,000 for a private taxi. One couple lost their valid return tickets this way. The only real govt tourist office is at 88 Janpath.

reports.title: Paharganj — sexual assault & drug risk for solo women
reports.desc: Paharganj is Wikipedia-documented for scams, drug peddling, and sexual assaults targeting women. Multiple women travellers have reported being followed, groped, and harassed in lanes especially after dark. Solo women should avoid Paharganj entirely — stay in South Delhi (Hauz Khas, Saket, Vasant Vihar) instead.

reports.title: Groping & exposure on Delhi Metro Yellow Line
reports.place: Delhi Metro Yellow Line (Saket to INA section)
reports.desc: A man exposed himself and groped a woman in a crowded Delhi Metro train. The Delhi court upheld the man's conviction in March 2026. Always use the dedicated women's coach (first coach in direction of travel). If in the general coach, stand near other women. Call 112 or alert CISF guards immediately.

reports.title: Harassment in women's coach on Magenta Line
reports.desc: A viral video from March 2026 showed 3–4 women harassing another woman inside the dedicated women's coach. The driverless Magenta Line has slower CISF response times. Even the women's coach isn't 100% safe. Sit near the coach entrance/exit, keep phone ready with 112 saved.

reports.title: Airport exit taxi overcharge — IGI Terminal 3
reports.desc: Touts immediately approach arriving travellers at T3 exit claiming Uber/Ola are unavailable and quoting 3–5× the real fare. A documented YouTube short (May 2025) shows scammers at T3 doing exactly this. Book Uber/Ola inside the terminal on the app BEFORE exiting. Prepaid taxi counter inside is the second option. Don't talk to anyone who approaches you outside.

reports.title: Hotel 'closed / burnt down' scam — airport & NDLS taxis
reports.place: IGI Airport arrivals and New Delhi Railway Station exits
reports.desc: Driver and sometimes a uniformed 'security guard' collude to convince you your booked hotel is closed, relocated, or full. They call a fake number ('the hotel') to confirm the lie, then take you to a commission hotel charging 3–5×. Always call your hotel directly on the booking number. Never accept this claim from strangers.

reports.title: Fake 'Government Tourist Office' touts — Connaught Place
reports.place: Connaught Place, near Rajiv Chowk Metro exit
reports.desc: Men approach tourists within minutes of exiting Rajiv Chowk Metro, tell you which block you're in (to start trust), then lead you to fake 'Govt Tourist Information' offices that sell overpriced or non-existent tours. Tripadvisor reviewers confirm this is the #1 scam at CP. Walk confidently, don't pause, don't make eye contact with touts.

reports.title: Auto-rickshaw diverted to commission shops
reports.place: Citywide — worst in Paharganj, CP, Old Delhi
reports.desc: Driver agrees to your destination then takes a 'shortcut' ending at a carpet/textile/jewellery shop where they earn commission. They claim the attraction is 'closed today' or suggest a stop first. Use Uber/Ola instead of hailing autos. If you must use auto, track on Google Maps and refuse any detour firmly.

reports.title: Fake train tickets sold outside Paharganj gate
reports.place: Paharganj side entrance, New Delhi Railway Station
reports.desc: Touts in and around the Paharganj entrance (including some in uniforms) sell unconfirmed or fully fake train tickets. Foreigners' Quota booking office is on the 2nd floor INSIDE the station — walk past all touts and go directly upstairs. Don't buy tickets from anyone outside the station.

reports.title: Eve-teasing (street harassment) at night near metro exits
reports.place: Citywide; worst at Rajiv Chowk, Kashmere Gate, Chandni Chowk after 9 PM
reports.desc: Women walking alone after dark near poorly-lit metro exits report cars/bikes slowing down, verbal abuse, and men following them. One woman interviewed by The Citizen said she carries stones in her hands when walking at night. Avoid being alone near metro exits after 9 PM. Book Uber/Ola directly to your next destination inside the metro station before exiting.

reports.title: Couchsurfing host danger for solo women
reports.place: Citywide — any private accommodation with unknown male host
reports.desc: Traveller Rachel Jones (HippieInHeels blog) documented contracting dengue, receiving fake train tickets, and being put in an unsafe situation by her Couchsurfing host in Delhi. Solo women should avoid staying with unknown men via CS or social platforms in Delhi. Use rated hostels or homestays with women hosts only.

reports.title: Fake entry tickets outside Qutub Minar
reports.desc: Sellers outside the monument gate sell 'entry tickets' that look legitimate but are rejected at the turnstile. Foreign tickets cost ₹550; local ₹35. Buy only at the official ASI counter inside, or book at asi.payumoney.com. Ignore anyone selling tickets on the street.

reports.title: Shoe-cleaning / polish scam — Connaught Place
reports.desc: A person kneels and smears polish or 'dye' on your shoes without consent, then demands ₹500–1,000 for 'cleaning'. Common enough that backpacker safety guides specifically call it out as a CP signature scam. If anyone touches your feet/shoes, walk away immediately before they finish.

reports.title: Currency exchange short-change at street kiosks
reports.place: Paharganj Market & small kiosks near major monuments
reports.desc: Exchanger counts bills slowly, distracts, then swaps your high-denomination note for a fake or lesser one. Only exchange at authorised bank branches or airport counters. Always count your money yourself before leaving the counter.

reports.title: 'Attraction is closed today' misdirection scam
reports.place: Outside Red Fort, India Gate, Qutub Minar
reports.desc: A friendly stranger (or even someone in unofficial uniform) tells you the monument is closed for a 'special event' or 'government function' and offers to take you to another place. Red Fort, India Gate, and Qutub Minar are almost never closed without advance public notice. Verify on the ASI website or call. Never follow strangers offering alternatives.

reports.title: Staring, unwanted photographing & following in Chandni Chowk
reports.desc: Solo women (especially foreign) report persistent staring, men walking alongside them uninvited, and covert phone photography in Chandni Chowk lanes. The narrow galis make escape difficult. Go in groups, dress conservatively (cover shoulders and knees), use women-only rickshaw services like Sakha Cabs if available, and avoid the narrow inner lanes after 6 PM.

reports.title: Pickpocket ring at Rajiv Chowk & Kashmere Gate metro
reports.place: Rajiv Chowk, Kashmere Gate, Chandni Chowk, NDLS metro stations
reports.desc: Delhi Police officially identified these among the top 12 pickpocket-prone metro stations. Organised gangs target distracted travellers in crowds. Keep your phone in a front pocket or money belt, bags zipped and worn in front. 3,679 mobile thefts were reported across Delhi Metro stations in a single year per police data.

reports.title: Gem export investment scam at tourist shops
reports.place: Shops near Connaught Place and tourist zones
reports.desc: A 'friendly local' offers to help you buy gems/stones cheaply claiming huge resale profit back home. The gems are worthless glass. Organised, multi-person setup with a convincing storefront. Never buy valuables from strangers or shops introduced by your driver/guide.

reports.title: Drugged food / drink from strangers on trains
reports.place: Trains departing from New Delhi Railway Station
reports.desc: Multiple reports describe strangers (sometimes seemingly friendly families) offering food, juice or tea on long-distance trains. Accepting can lead to robbery after the victim loses consciousness. Documented enough for solo-travel guides to specifically warn women. Never accept food or drink from strangers on trains — bring your own.

reports.title: Unwanted touching on local DTC buses
reports.place: DTC city buses, especially crowded routes
reports.desc: Solo women report groping and deliberate pushing on crowded DTC local buses. Unlike the metro, DTC buses have no women-only section and minimal enforcement. Solo women travellers are advised to avoid DTC local buses entirely — use Delhi Metro (women's coach) or Uber/Ola instead.

reports.title: Fake Wi-Fi & ATM card cloning at tourist spots
reports.place: Near major monuments and tourist markets citywide
reports.desc: Fake public Wi-Fi networks steal login credentials. ATM skimmers are planted on machines near tourist zones. Use only bank-branded ATMs inside banks during business hours. Never connect to unnamed public Wi-Fi. Use your mobile data for any banking.

reports.title: Hotel staff harassment in Paharganj budget hotels
reports.desc: Multiple solo female travellers report male hotel staff knocking on room doors late at night, entering without permission, and propositioning women. One solo-traveller blog documents this firsthand. Women should lock doors with an additional travel lock, avoid ground-floor rooms, and choose female-reviewed stays in South Delhi instead.

reports.title: Tuk-tuk driver shopping-commission detour
reports.place: Near Jama Masjid, Red Fort, Old Delhi
reports.desc: Driver agrees to take you to Jama Masjid / Red Fort but stops at a 'must-see' carpet, pashmina, or marble shop en route. If you buy nothing, the driver may demand extra fare or become hostile. Agree on fare upfront, state clearly 'no shopping stops', track on Google Maps, use Uber instead.

reports.title: Stalking after sharing hotel name with strangers
reports.place: Citywide — risk starts at airport / station
reports.desc: Women who share their hotel name with helpful-seeming strangers (at airport, in autos, at monuments) have been followed or found that strangers appear at their hotel. Never tell strangers which hotel you're staying in. Give only a general area ('South Delhi') if asked.

reports.title: Inflated auto-rickshaw fare (meter refused)
reports.place: Citywide — worst outside NDLS, Paharganj, CP
reports.desc: Auto drivers refuse to use meters and quote ₹300–500 for a ₹80–100 ride. Some tamper meters. GoAuto and InDrive apps give fixed fares. Alternatively use Delhi Metro + Ola/Uber for last mile. Always negotiate hard before getting in if taking street autos and verify price on Google Maps.

reports.title: Night-walking risk near Connaught Place after 10 PM
reports.desc: Connaught Place — while well-lit — attracts aggressive men targeting solo women at night. One Quora account from a Delhi local describes 'goonda' openly harassing elderly women at CP, walking in the same direction, with police absent. Women should not walk alone at CP after 10 PM. Pre-book Uber/Ola from wherever you are.

reports.title: Fake booking websites mimicking Delhi hotels
reports.place: Online — risk starts before you arrive
reports.desc: Fake hotel-booking websites designed to look like real hotel sites or aggregators collected an estimated ₹47 crore from tourists in 2025 per cyber-crime data. Always book through MakeMyTrip, Booking.com, or the hotel's verified direct number. Check the URL carefully — fake sites use near-identical domains.

reports.title: Women harassed in Lodi Garden after dusk
reports.desc: Lodi Garden is beautiful by day but becomes a risk zone for solo women after dark. Isolated paths, low police presence, and groups have reported unwanted male attention toward solo women visitors. Leave before sunset. Go with company during the day. Don't visit after 5:30 PM in winter, 7 PM in summer.

reports.title: Overpriced souvenir traps in Chandni Chowk market
reports.desc: Vendors quote 5–10× the local price to tourists, especially foreign women travelling alone. A small item locals buy for ₹50 is quoted ₹500. Persistent refusal to bargain, guilt-tripping, and emotional pressure tactics are common. Start negotiations at 20–30% of the quoted price and be prepared to walk away.

reports.title: Pickpocketing on local trains & at CST
reports.desc: Mumbai's #1 tourist crime. Front pocket, zipped bag, no phone in back pocket.

reports.title: Fake police at Colaba / Gateway
reports.desc: Demand 'foreigner check' + on-spot fine. Demand to go to police station instead.

reports.title: Gem & carpet shops in Colaba
reports.desc: Same export scam, slightly less aggressive than Jaipur.

reports.title: Tourist boat overcharge to Elephanta
reports.desc: Official Elephanta ferry is ₹220–270 from Gateway. Touts quote ₹500–800.

reports.title: Photo + 'small donation' at Gateway
reports.desc: Photographer gives 'free' photo, then asks for ₹500.

reports.title: Hotel switch from airport
reports.desc: Less common than Delhi but still happens at international arrivals after midnight.

reports.title: Bottled water tampering near beaches
reports.desc: Check seal, prefer chain stores.

reports.title: Hotel Switch / 'Your hotel burned down'
reports.desc: Taxi/auto driver claims your booked hotel is closed, burned, overbooked, or in a 'riot zone'. Drives you to a partner hotel paying 30–50% commission. Currently most active at IGI Airport T3 (after midnight arrivals) and New Delhi Railway Station.

reports.title: Mumbai Local women-only compartments
reports.desc: First-class ladies coach is the safest way to travel. Off-peak hours quietest. Avoid 8–10am and 5–8pm rush in general compartment.

reports.title: Priyadarshini Taxi — women-driver service
reports.desc: Verified women drivers. Pre-book via phone or Uber Women-only Bike Rides via app. ₹150–400 typical city ride.

reports.title: Gem export scam (the legendary one)
reports.desc: Friendly local → 'family jeweller' → 'export gems for profit' → glass beads. Lose lakhs.

reports.title: Textile / block print 'family workshop'
reports.desc: Driver commissions you to specific shop. Prices 5–10x normal Bapu Bazaar rates.

reports.title: 'Friendly student' approaches at Hawa Mahal
reports.desc: Sets up the gem scam. Walk away from anyone unprompted.

reports.title: Amer Fort 'closed' / wrong gate
reports.desc: Touts redirect you to a paid 'alternate viewing point' = commission shop.

reports.title: Auto charging by 'tourist rate'
reports.desc: Use Ola/Uber/Rapido. Auto from station to old city = ₹150 by app vs ₹500 quoted.

reports.title: Fake palace tour packages
reports.desc: WhatsApp groups offering ₹500 'all monuments tour' drop you at commission shops.

reports.title: Elephant ride at Amer overcharge & welfare issue
reports.desc: Govt rate is fixed (~₹1,100). Touts charge ₹3,000+. Also: ethical concerns documented by PETA India.

reports.title: Hotel Switch / 'Your hotel burned down'
reports.desc: Taxi/auto driver claims your booked hotel is closed, burned, overbooked, or in a 'riot zone'. Drives you to a partner hotel paying 30–50% commission. Currently most active at IGI Airport T3 (after midnight arrivals) and New Delhi Railway Station.

reports.title: Pink City Rickshaw Co — women drivers
reports.desc: Women drivers from low-income backgrounds. Heritage walks + day rentals. ₹400–800/day. Book at official kiosk near Hawa Mahal or call.

reports.title: Auto-rickshaw drops you at wrong Galta Ji entrance
reports.desc: Drivers drop tourists at the back hill instead of the main gate, then demand extra cash for the 'short route' or turn around to overcharge. Walk through Galta Gate from the city side or verify on Google Maps before boarding.

reports.title: Fake gemstone resale scheme by motorcycle youth
reports.place: Near City Palace and tourist hotels
reports.desc: Young men on motorbikes approach foreigners claiming they want to 'practise English' or 'understand Western culture'. After gaining trust over tea, they pitch a gemstone resale scam — buy fake stones cheap, sell at huge profit at home. Gems are worthless glass. A US tourist lost ₹6 crore in a documented 2024 case.

reports.title: Taxi driver claims monument is closed, redirects to commission shop
reports.place: Amber Fort, City Palace, Hawa Mahal — Jaipur-wide
reports.desc: Driver insists your destination is 'closed today for a ceremony/maintenance' and takes you to a textile or gem shop where they earn 30–40% commission. Always verify timings on the official Rajasthan Tourism site before trusting any driver.

reports.title: Inflated auto-rickshaw fares from Jaipur Junction
reports.desc: Drivers outside the main exit quote ₹500–800 for rides that cost ₹100–150 via Ola/Uber. They refuse meters and target confused new arrivals with luggage. Use Ola/Uber from inside the station or the official prepaid taxi counter near the exit gate.

reports.title: Fake 'Government Tourist Office' on Amber Fort road
reports.desc: Shops with official-looking signs saying 'India Tourism' or 'Government Tourist Information' sell overpriced tour packages, fake composite tickets, and IRCTC bookings with huge commissions. The only legitimate India Tourism office does NOT sell tickets. Book directly at monument counters or rajasthantourism.gov.in.

reports.title: Fake Pashminas sold inside City Palace shops
reports.desc: Small shops within the City Palace complex sell synthetic fabric labelled as 'genuine Pashmina'. TripAdvisor users report paying premium prices for cheap acrylic. Always buy Pashmina from GI-tagged retailers with written certification.

reports.title: Razai (quilt) sold near Amer Fort at 5× bazaar price
reports.desc: Tourists have paid ₹2,000–4,000 for Jaipuri razais (quilts) from shops adjacent to Amber Fort, only to find identical items in Bapu Bazaar or Johari Bazaar for ₹400–800. Never buy large items from tour-guide-recommended shops near monuments.

reports.title: Hawa Mahal photo spot lures tourists into commission shop
reports.desc: A shopkeeper invites tourists to photograph Hawa Mahal from his building's balcony — a great vantage point. Once you finish photos, he harasses you to buy goods and turns aggressive on refusal. Multiple TripAdvisor reports confirm this. Rooftop cafés nearby offer the same view legitimately.

reports.title: Rickshaw claims temples are 'entry restricted' without traditional clothes
reports.desc: Drivers tell tourists jeans/Western clothing are banned at specific temples and you must buy traditional clothes — from a shop they know. No standard Jaipur temple bans Western dress (modest coverage is all that's needed). Ignore this entirely.

reports.title: Fake tickets sold outside Amber Fort by touts
reports.desc: Touts near the fort entrance sell 'discounted' entry tickets that are either invalid or grossly overpriced versus the official counter. Official foreign tourist entry is ₹550 (2025). Only buy at the official ASI / Rajasthan Tourism counter inside the gate.

reports.title: Auto-rickshaw driver befriends, then sells fake gold worth ₹29 lakh
reports.desc: Documented 2024 case: an auto driver befriended a Japanese tourist, offered a ₹600 city tour, introduced him to associates, and collectively sold fake gold worth ₹29 lakh. Three suspects arrested by Jaipur Tourist Police. Never make large purchases with new 'friends' you met via your driver.

reports.title: Elephant ride overcharge at Amer Fort + serious safety risk
reports.desc: Touts charge ₹1,500–2,500 per couple for elephant rides where the official rate is ~₹1,100. Beyond overcharging, PETA documented TB-infected elephants still being used in 2024. A tourist was grabbed and swung by an elephant trunk (Feb 2024). Avoid entirely — walk or take the official jeep (₹450 / 5 pax).

reports.title: Aggressive monkeys steal food and belongings at Galta Ji
reports.desc: Hundreds of rhesus macaques at Galta Ji are bold and trained by handouts. They snatch bags, sunglasses, water bottles, and food from hands. Don't feed them, don't show visible snacks, and keep bags zipped and tucked in front. Visit before 11 AM to avoid peak heat-aggression.

reports.title: Bazaar vendor swaps your large note with a fake one
reports.place: Bapu Bazaar, Johari Bazaar, Nehru Bazaar
reports.desc: Vendor quickly palms your ₹500 or ₹2,000 note and replaces it with a fake, claiming you gave a counterfeit. Happens in high-distraction moments with multiple items on the table. Always count change in hand, pay in smaller denominations, and use UPI/card when possible.

reports.title: Milk/food begging scam leads to overpriced shop
reports.desc: Women with babies or children approach tourists asking not for money but for milk or food. They lead you to a nearby shop (run by an associate), then hand the items back for resale after you pay 3–5× market rate. If you want to help, donate directly to verified NGOs — not via shops.

reports.title: Overpriced souvenir shops in Johari Bazaar targeting foreigners
reports.desc: Market vendors quote 'tourist prices' 5–10× the local rate for jewellery, textiles, and handicrafts. Starting at 30% of the quoted price is standard practice. Always compare prices at multiple shops and avoid buying from the first place a guide or driver takes you.

reports.title: Low-quality gemstones sold as 'certified export quality'
reports.place: Gem shops citywide (highest risk near Amber Fort road)
reports.desc: Jaipur is India's biggest gemstone hub — and its biggest gemstone scam hub. Synthetic, glass, or low-grade stones passed off as sapphires, rubies, and emeralds with fake certificates. Only buy from shops listed by the GJEPC and insist on third-party lab certification.

reports.title: Travel agent forces commission shopping stops during tour
reports.desc: Guides and drivers earn 30–40% commission from textile shops, gem stores, and handicraft emporiums. Even paying customers get taken on unplanned shopping detours. Tell your guide/driver upfront you will NOT visit any shops; if they deviate, document and report to your hotel or the tourist helpline (1800-11-1363).

reports.title: Fake train ticket agents near Jaipur Junction charge 50% markup
reports.desc: Touts posing as travel agents outside the station charge massive premiums to book train tickets, sometimes providing invalid tickets entirely. Always book trains through the official IRCTC website (irctc.co.in) or the official ticket windows inside the station.

reports.title: Pani puri water causes serious food poisoning in tourists
reports.place: Street food stalls, Old City & bazaars
reports.desc: Pani puri stalls widely use tap or unfiltered water in their flavoured liquid — a documented vector for E. coli, typhoid, and gastroenteritis. Foreign visitors are especially susceptible. Eat pani puri only at established restaurants with bottled/RO water. Avoid all unbottled drinks from street stalls.

reports.title: Rickshaw takes scenic route to inflate fare
reports.desc: Drivers take intentionally longer routes to inflate the trip cost. Simple fix: open Google Maps navigation mode from the moment you board. Drivers know you can see the route and won't detour if monitored. This eliminates the vast majority of route scams.

reports.title: 'Hotel full / closed' switch at Jaipur airport drop-off
reports.place: Jaipur International Airport (JAI), arrivals & city roads
reports.desc: Drivers (even pre-booked) claim your hotel is 'under renovation', 'closed for a function', or 'doesn't exist' and redirect you to an alternative hotel paying them commission. Call your hotel directly before getting in any vehicle. Your booked hotel will confirm real-time availability.

reports.title: Overpriced camel/horse photo charge after the snap is taken
reports.place: Amber Fort road, City Palace area
reports.desc: Handlers lead decorated camels or horses near tourists and encourage 'just one photo'. Once photographed (or even just approached), they demand ₹500–1,000 per person aggressively. If you didn't agree to a fee before engaging, walk away firmly — you have no legal obligation to pay.

reports.title: Shoe-keeper charges or swaps shoes at temple entrances
reports.place: Birla Mandir, Govind Dev Ji & other major temples
reports.desc: Unofficial 'shoe minders' collect your footwear at temple gates and demand ₹50–200 per pair on return, or return a different pair claiming a mix-up. Leave cheaper sandals and carry valuables in. Use designated official shoe counters only (usually free or ₹5–10).

reports.title: Pickpocketing gangs on crowded buses and at monuments
reports.place: Public buses, Hawa Mahal, Bapu Bazaar
reports.desc: Pickpocket groups (sometimes involving children or women) target fatigued tourists on buses and in crowded markets. Don't keep wallets in back pockets. Use a money belt or front-zipped pouch. Never fall asleep on public buses with bags unattended.

reports.title: Avoid lone night walks in Old City and unlit streets
reports.place: Pink City (Old City), Jaipur — after 9 PM
reports.desc: Post-9 PM, many narrow lanes in the old walled city are poorly lit and secluded. Solo travellers, especially women, face higher risks of harassment and petty crime. Stick to MI Road, C-Scheme, and well-lit commercial areas at night. Use Ola/Uber rather than walking.

reports.title: Free 'English practice' student leads to multi-day gem investment scam
reports.desc: A very elaborate multi-day scam: a 'student' befriends a foreign tourist, earns trust, then introduces a 'tax problem' angle where the tourist must carry gems through customs for a cut. Even if gems are posted in front of you, you'll never see real value or returns. One of India's oldest and most sophisticated tourist cons.

reports.title: Tap water is unsafe — don't drink even in hotels
reports.desc: Jaipur's tap water is not potable for tourists. Municipal supply contains high TDS and bacterial contamination. Drink only sealed bottled water (check the seal before opening) or boiled/filtered water. Brush teeth with bottled water. Avoid ice in drinks from street vendors.

reports.title: Composite ticket duplicate sold outside monument gates
reports.place: Amber Fort, Hawa Mahal, Jantar Mantar gates
reports.desc: Touts sell 'discounted' composite tickets that are invalid, expired, or counterfeit. The Jaipur Composite Ticket (valid for Amber Fort, Hawa Mahal, Jantar Mantar, Albert Hall and more) costs ₹400 for foreigners. Buy ONLY at official counters inside the first monument you visit or at rajasthantourism.gov.in.

reports.title: Fake charity collectors targeting tourists at traffic lights and markets
reports.place: MI Road, Bapu Bazaar, major intersections
reports.desc: Men and women approach tourists with printed sheets claiming to collect for schools, orphanages, or flood victims. Receipts look official but the organisations are unregistered. Never donate to street collectors. If you want to give, use verified NGOs like GiveIndia (giveindia.org) or Akshaya Patra.

reports.title: 'Taj Mahal closed today' / fake holiday
reports.desc: Touts near gates. Taj is closed only on FRIDAYS — verify yourself at the ticket counter.

reports.title: Fake guides at Taj Mahal
reports.desc: Demand ₹2,000–₹5,000 after free 'tour'. Hire only ASI-licensed guides with photo ID at the counter.

reports.title: Marble inlay / gem export scam
reports.desc: Drivers commission you to shops claiming 'family workshop'. Same scam as Jaipur — overpriced fakes.

reports.title: Auto/taxi inflated fares from Cantt station
reports.desc: Use prepaid taxi at station OR Uber/Ola. Quoted rates are 3–5x real.

reports.title: Photographer at Taj demanding fees
reports.desc: Approach you with 'free photo' offer, then demand ₹500–₹2,000 per print.

reports.title: Shoe-cover fee scam
reports.desc: Free shoe covers come with your Taj ticket. Don't pay anyone selling them.

reports.title: Hotel Switch / 'Your hotel burned down'
reports.desc: Taxi/auto driver claims your booked hotel is closed, burned, overbooked, or in a 'riot zone'. Drives you to a partner hotel paying 30–50% commission. Currently most active at IGI Airport T3 (after midnight arrivals) and New Delhi Railway Station.

reports.title: Fake Government Tourist Office
reports.desc: Fake offices with 'Govt. of India Tourism' signage sell inflated tour packages, fake train tickets, or non-existent hotel bookings. The real India Tourism office is at 88 Janpath Road, Delhi. Real IRCTC International Tourist Bureau is on the 1st floor of New Delhi Railway Stat…

reports.title: Buy Taj tickets online at asi.payumoney.com
reports.desc: Avoids the 200+ touts at the gate selling fake or marked-up tickets. Foreign tourist ₹1,100, India ₹50. QR scan at gate. Skip the queue too.

reports.title: Fake website for Taj Mahal ticket booking
reports.desc: Cybercriminals built ticketstajmahal.com to collect money for bogus Taj Mahal e-tickets. ASI officially warned tourists. Only book on asiagracircle.in or tajmahal.gov.in.

reports.title: Fake government guide with forged ASI ID at South Gate
reports.desc: Man in khaki uniform showed laminated fake ASI card and charged ₹2,500 for a tour — real rate is ₹500–1,500. Real ASI guides don't approach tourists; hire only at the ASI ticket counter inside.

reports.title: Auto-rickshaw took 8 km detour for ₹600 ride
reports.place: Agra Cantt Station to Taj Mahal East Gate
reports.desc: Agreed ₹200 for the ride. Driver took the long route via Sadar Bazaar and demanded ₹600 at drop. Actual distance is 4 km, Ola/Uber charges ₹80–120. Use an app cab from the station exit.

reports.title: Taj Mahal 'closed today' lie near West Gate
reports.place: Taj Mahal West Gate approach road
reports.desc: Rickshaw driver and 'friendly local' insisted the Taj was closed for a government function and offered to take us to a carpet shop. Taj was fully open. Always verify on tajmahal.gov.in.

reports.title: Fake marble inlay swapped with soap replica
reports.place: Marble emporium near East Gate, Taj Nagri Phase 2
reports.desc: Verified marble under UV light in shop, paid ₹4,800. Got home to find a painted soap replica — swapped during wrapping. Only buy from UP Tourism-approved shops. Real Makrana marble starts ₹3,000.

reports.title: Fake prepaid taxi counter at Agra Cantt station
reports.place: Agra Cantt Railway Station — exit hall
reports.desc: Unofficial counter in yellow vest quoted ₹800 for Taj Mahal (5 km). Official prepaid booth charges ₹180. The fake counter has no government signage. The official one says 'UPSRTC Pre-Paid Taxi' on green signage.

reports.title: Pickpocket gang at Taj Mahal security queue
reports.place: Taj Mahal West Gate security screening line
reports.desc: Two men faked an argument ahead of us, a third unzipped my bag from behind. Lost ₹3,000 cash. The security queue is the most-targeted spot. Keep bags in front and zip every pocket before joining the line.

reports.title: Fatehpur Sikri: forced guide at parking lot
reports.place: Fatehpur Sikri parking, ~1 km from monument entrance
reports.desc: Men block the path at the parking lot claiming vehicles aren't allowed and a guide is mandatory — both lies. Walk freely to the ticketed entrance (₹500 foreigners) and hire guides inside only.

reports.title: VIP 'skip the line' ticket sold outside the gate
reports.place: Taj Mahal West Gate road, by vendor carts
reports.desc: Man sold laminated 'VIP fast-track' tickets for ₹2,200 claiming they skip security. No such ticket exists — these are fakes or old reused tickets. Turned away at entry. Book only on the official ASI site.

reports.title: Tuk-tuk 'Agra tour package' mid-ride price hike
reports.desc: Agreed ₹600 for full-monument tour. Driver demanded extra 'parking fees' and 'waiting charges' at each stop. Final bill ₹1,800. Negotiate an ALL-IN fixed price up front, including waiting time.

reports.title: Agra Fort guide doubles fee inside the monument
reports.place: Agra Fort — Amar Singh Gate, inside
reports.desc: Hired guide outside the gate for ₹200, he demanded ₹800 more midway through or threatened to leave us stranded. Never hire guides outside the gate — use the official booth at the entry counter.

reports.title: Fake gem investment pitch near hotel
reports.place: Taj Ganj area — approached on the street
reports.desc: Man offered to help buy wholesale gems to 'resell at profit back home,' took us to his cousin's shop. Gems were glass. This classic scam is common in Agra. Walk away immediately from any such approach.

reports.title: Charity donation pressure near Taj Mahal entry
reports.place: Pathway from East Gate parking to ticket counter
reports.desc: Children with donation books claim to be from a school for blind kids, showing signatures suggesting ₹500–₹2,000 donations. The 'school' doesn't exist. Don't engage or sign anything.

reports.title: Tourist restaurant charged 10× menu price
reports.place: Rooftop café on Taj East Gate Road, Tajganj
reports.desc: Menu showed ₹120 for chai, ₹250 for dal makhani. Bill came to ₹1,800 with unexplained 'tourist tax' and 40% service charge. Check pricing carefully; restaurants 5 min away in Sadar Bazaar are fair.

reports.title: Tap water served as 'mineral water' in budget hotels
reports.desc: Hotel provided 'free mineral water' that was tap water in resealed bottles — seal broke without the standard click. Several guests got severe diarrhoea. Always press the neck ring; buy sealed Bisleri from shops.

reports.title: Rickshaw commission loop: hotel → 'closed' attraction → shop
reports.place: Taj Ganj — starts at hotel pickup
reports.desc: Told driver to go to Itmad-ud-Daulah (Baby Taj). He said it was 'closed for renovation' and drove to a carpet showroom. Check opening hours on asi.nic.in first. If a driver says a monument is closed, get out and verify.

reports.title: Fatehpur Sikri Sufi shrine donation demand
reports.place: Dargah of Sheikh Salim Chishti, Fatehpur Sikri
reports.desc: Man tied a thread on my wrist, chanted prayer, demanded ₹2,000 as 'mandatory Sufi donation' — became aggressive on refusal. Firmly say 'nahin chahiye' before anyone touches you. You are not obligated to pay.

reports.title: Photographer charges upfront, delivers blurry phone photos
reports.desc: Photographer offered DSLR portraits for ₹500, then demanded ₹1,500 'for editing.' Delivered 5 grainy phone photos. Agree on exact price, delivery format, and number of photos BEFORE any shot is taken.

reports.title: Fake ASI tourist information office near Agra Cantt
reports.place: Shop opposite Agra Cantt railway station
reports.desc: Looks official with ASI logo and uniformed staff. Sold fake 'government-approved Agra tour package' with bogus priority Taj tickets for ₹4,500. Real ASI offices are inside monuments only. Any 'tourist office' near a station is fraudulent.

reports.title: Yamuna boat ride: quoted ₹200, billed ₹1,800
reports.desc: Boatmen quoted ₹200 for sunset Taj view from the river. On return demanded ₹1,800 saying the price was per person, per hour, each way. Agree on the TOTAL group price for both ways before boarding — get it in writing.

reports.title: Bag slashed on cycle-rickshaw in Kinari Bazaar
reports.desc: Person walking beside the rickshaw cut through my backpack side pocket and took ₹4,000 cash. Happens frequently in narrow market lanes. Use slash-proof crossbody bags. Kinari Bazaar is very high-risk.

reports.title: Severe food poisoning from street kulfi near East Gate
reports.place: Kulfi stalls, Shilpgram area near Taj East Gate
reports.desc: Kulfi made with unfiltered water caused severe vomiting within 4 hours. Three of our group hospitalised overnight. Avoid roadside ice cream, lassi and cut fruit near the Taj. Eat only packaged products or go to Pind Balluchi.

reports.title: Agra Fort unofficial guide demands money mid-tour
reports.place: Agra Fort — Amar Singh Gate outer area
reports.desc: Man gives a free 5-minute intro outside the gate, then follows you inside uninvited and demands ₹3,000 at the end for 'tour services.' This is extortion. Don't engage anyone outside the gate who speaks first.

reports.title: Hotel sold fake 'private moonrise Taj viewing' tickets
reports.place: Hotel in Tajganj — sold by front desk
reports.desc: Hotel sold 'exclusive moonrise viewing' tickets for ₹2,200 each. Took us to a rooftop 700 m away with no ASI access. Official Taj night viewing (full moon only) is booked on the Taj Mahal website at ₹750 for foreigners.

reports.title: Currency exchange: short-changed with torn notes
reports.place: Informal money changers near Sadar Bazaar
reports.desc: Exchanged USD 100, received ₹7,200 instead of ₹8,300+. Bundle included 4 torn / taped notes shops refused. Use only bank ATMs or RBI-authorised exchange counters. Never change money at street shops.

reports.title: Rickshaw drops at fake 'UP Government Marble Emporium'
reports.desc: Taken to a private shop named 'UP Government Marble Emporium' with certificates on walls. Prices 5–10× market rate. Driver earns ₹200–500 commission per tourist. Real govt-approved shops are listed on uptourism.gov.in.

reports.title: Heatstroke risk: 700 m unshaded walk to Taj East Gate
reports.place: Taj Mahal — East Gate parking to ticket counter
reports.desc: April–June temperatures exceed 45°C and the battery bus is often 'not running.' The 700 m walk in direct sun caused one in our group to faint. Carry 1.5 L water, wear a hat, visit before 8 am. Battery bus is free with a foreign ticket — insist on it.

reports.title: Children offer flower garlands then demand ₹500
reports.place: Taj Mahal West Gate outer road
reports.desc: Children drape garlands on tourists without asking, then demand ₹500. If you photograph them, adults appear demanding money for 'photo rights.' Decline all unsolicited flower / garland offers immediately and firmly.

reports.title: Delhi-Agra tour bus stops at stone carving factory for 90 min
reports.place: Stone inlay factory on Fatehabad Road
reports.desc: Day tour included an unannounced 90-minute stop at a sales showroom with heavy pressure to buy. Guides earned commissions. Inlay prices were 4× market rate. When booking Delhi-Agra tours, ask explicitly if factory visits are included.

reports.title: Fake IRCTC tourist counter sold invalid train tickets
reports.place: Outside Agra Cantt Railway Station, left side exit
reports.desc: Unofficial counter called 'IRCTC Tourist Cell' sold Agra-Delhi tickets for ₹700 each — they were fake printouts. Caught at the platform gate. Real IRCTC only via the irctc.co.in app or inside the station's official reservation hall (blue signage).

reports.title: Auto rickshaw refusing meter
reports.desc: Most rampant scam in Bangalore. ALWAYS use Rapido/Uber/Namma Yatri (official BMTC-backed app).

reports.title: Fake driver at airport claiming to be your Uber/Ola
reports.desc: Verify license plate before getting in.

reports.title: Fake brand stores in Commercial Street
reports.desc: Counterfeit Apple/Samsung accessories. Buy electronics at authorised stores only.

reports.title: ATM skimming in standalone street ATMs
reports.desc: Use only HDFC/ICICI/SBI/Axis branch ATMs.

reports.title: Pickpocketing at Majestic during festivals
reports.desc: Especially during Dussehra, Diwali.

reports.title: Fake job/visa scam targeting foreign workers
reports.desc: Recent rise — affects African and SE Asian tourists.

reports.title: Pre-paid Taxi Diversion / Fake Driver
reports.desc: After paying at the official prepaid counter, driver claims he doesn't know your address, the road is closed, or there's a strike. Detours to commission shops or drops you at wrong hotel. Variant: a tout at the airport claims to be your booked Uber/Ola driver.

reports.title: Bird Poop / Stained Shirt Distraction
reports.desc: Stranger points out 'something' on your shirt; accomplice 'helps clean' while picking your pocket of phone/wallet.

reports.title: Uber Women — women drivers, women riders only
reports.desc: Launched Dec 2024. Book via Uber app. Women-only driver matched with women-only rider. ₹50–200 typical. Available 24/7 across the city.

reports.title: Namma Metro is safe — women-only coach
reports.desc: First coach reserved for women on every train. CCTV throughout. Stations well-lit and staffed until 11pm. Best way to skip evening traffic safely.

reports.title: Fake Uber app at KIA airport — Blumeter fraud
reports.desc: Driver used a fake app called 'Blumeter' that looked identical to Uber, overcharging by ₹1,000+. Claimed extra charges were GST. Refused to give a receipt, said the billing system was 'broken'. Only use official Uber/Ola booked inside the terminal before exiting.

reports.title: Airport taxi syndicate — double the fare, fake receipt
reports.desc: Woman charged ₹2,308 for a 41 km ride to JP Nagar (normal ₹1,000–₹1,400). Driver gave a tampered digital receipt with wrong details. Airport staff confirmed an internal taxi syndicate is operating. Use BIAL's official prepaid counter or book Uber/Ola from the app inside the terminal.

reports.title: Ola driver demands ₹200 toll cash — threatens abandonment
reports.place: Kalyan Nagar to KIA airport route
reports.desc: Passenger booked an Ola cab with toll included in the app fare. Driver demanded ₹200 extra cash for toll and threatened to leave the passenger on the road if refused. Screenshot the fare breakdown before the trip begins.

reports.title: Auto-rickshaw refuses meter — tourist hotspot overcharging
reports.place: MG Road, Brigade Road, ISKCON, Bangalore Palace
reports.desc: Auto drivers routinely refuse to run meters near tourist zones and quote 3–5× actual fares. Common at MG Road, Brigade Road, Koramangala, and outside major temples. Use Namma Metro, Uber, or Ola instead. If you must take an auto, agree on fare via the app or insist on the meter before getting in.

reports.title: Private cab shows fake Uber invoice — adds fake night charges
reports.desc: Passenger booked a private airport cab but the driver showed a fake Uber-branded invoice and added unauthorised late-night surcharges that weren't agreed upon. Always book directly through Uber/Ola apps and screenshot the fare estimate before riding.

reports.title: Fake UPI payment app used by gang — shops cheated
reports.place: Yelahanka New Town shops and restaurants
reports.desc: Gang in masks scanned merchant QR codes using a fake app mimicking PhonePe, showed a false 'payment successful' screen, and left without paying. A mobile shop lost ₹15,000 (OnePlus earbuds purchase); a biryani restaurant also targeted. Always wait for the SMS / bank notification before releasing goods — never trust the screen shown by the buyer.

reports.title: BMTC bus conductor replaces official QR with personal UPI code
reports.desc: Several BMTC conductors removed official BMTC QR codes from buses and showed their own personal UPI QR codes instead — fares went directly to private accounts. Tourists and non-Kannada speakers were the easiest targets. Until new ETM machines roll out, confirm UPI payment went to 'BMTC' and insist on a printed ticket. Four conductors suspended.

reports.title: Fake QR code stickers — malicious code redirects payment
reports.desc: Scammers place fake QR stickers over legitimate merchant codes. When you scan, money goes to the fraudster's account, not the merchant. UPI losses nationally hit ₹485 crore in FY 2024-25. Always verify the UPI recipient name on your app before confirming payment.

reports.title: Fake licensed guides at Bengaluru Fort and Dodda Ganapathi
reports.place: Bengaluru Fort, Dodda Ganapathi Temple, heritage sites
reports.desc: Unlicensed 'guides' approach tourists claiming to be official Karnataka tourism guides. They give inaccurate information and lead visitors to commission-based shops. Confirmed widespread by Deccan Herald (Apr 2026). Ask to see a valid government-issued guide license before hiring.

reports.title: Commission shop trap — auto driver takes you to 'cousin's shop'
reports.place: Citywide — Commercial Street, MG Road area
reports.desc: Auto-rickshaw or cab driver offers to show you around then insists on a detour to a shop (textiles, souvenirs, gems) where he gets 20–30% commission. Prices are heavily inflated. Politely refuse any detours not on your itinerary.

reports.title: Forced donations at Shivoham Shiva Temple — unofficial collectors
reports.place: Shivoham Shiva Temple, 97 Old Airport Rd, Murgesh Pallya
reports.desc: Unofficial collection agents inside the temple ignore queue-based devotees and pressure visitors to buy 'special darshan' tickets (₹500/person). Multiple Tripadvisor reviews confirm this is systematic. Donation to God is voluntary — don't feel pressured.

reports.title: Mobile phone snatching at MG Road and Brigade Road — New Year crowds
reports.place: MG Road, Church Street, Brigade Road
reports.desc: Over 10 mobile phones stolen in a single night during New Year 2024 celebrations on MG Road and Brigade Road. Cases registered under Ashok Nagar and Cubbon Park police stations. These streets get dangerously crowded during festivals and nights — keep phones in front pockets or bags held in front of your body.

reports.title: Chain snatching on motorbikes — women targeted citywide
reports.place: Peenya, Yeshwanthpur, residential streets across Bengaluru
reports.desc: Two men on a stolen motorbike tail women wearing gold jewellery, ram into them, snatch chains/mangalsutra and flee. A single accused had 70+ cases across Karnataka and Tamil Nadu. Bengaluru police describe these as occurring with 'metronomic regularity'. Don't wear visible gold jewellery when walking or in autos.

reports.title: Pickpockets at Namma Metro busy stations
reports.desc: Several pickpockets arrested at busy Namma Metro stations in 2024–2025. Thieves target rush-hour crowds when coaches are packed. Keep wallet, phone, and valuables in front pockets or zipped bags. Be especially vigilant when boarding or alighting — that's when jostling provides cover.

reports.title: Two-wheeler theft — 14–16 stolen per day in Bengaluru
reports.place: Parking areas citywide — malls, IT parks, residential
reports.desc: 3,263 two-wheelers stolen Jan–Aug 2024 with only 779 recovered. Thieves use stolen bikes for chain snatching and other crimes, then sell them with fake registration documents on OLX. Never leave valuables in your two-wheeler or rental scooter. Use a disc lock + chain in addition to the factory lock.

reports.title: Deadly potholes — 539 fatal road accidents Jan–Aug 2025
reports.place: Citywide — especially Dr Rajkumar Road and residential roads
reports.desc: Bengaluru recorded 3,105 road accidents in Jan–Aug 2025, of which 539 were fatal (558 deaths). In early 2025 a man died and a 70-year-old was killed on Dr Rajkumar Road after hitting road trenches. Bengaluru topped India's list for pedestrian deaths. Walk on footpaths where available; cross only at marked crossings; avoid walking on roads after dark.

reports.title: Drivers do not stop for pedestrians — crossing is dangerous
reports.place: All major roads, especially areas without pedestrian infrastructure
reports.desc: Bengaluru has virtually no pedestrian-first culture. Drivers rarely stop at crossings unless physically forced to. In 2023 the city topped India for pedestrian deaths (292 fatalities — ~10% of the 53-city total). Cross in small groups, make eye contact with drivers, and never assume a car will stop.

reports.title: Sewage mixed with tap water — typhoid and cholera outbreaks
reports.desc: In Nov 2024, 300 BTM Layout residents faced health risks when sewage entered Cauvery water lines. In Feb 2025 a BWSSB pipeline leak caused sewage mixing in southern suburbs — hundreds contracted typhoid and diarrhoea. E. coli and Klebsiella found in Cauvery water samples. Never drink tap water in Bengaluru. Use only sealed bottled water from known brands.

reports.title: Cholera surge — 40% increase in cases in April 2024
reports.desc: April 2024 saw a 40% spike in cholera cases in Bengaluru. Multiple localities reported sick residents. WHO has previously flagged Bengaluru's potable water as more contaminated than Delhi, Mumbai or Chennai. Avoid ice cubes, raw salads washed in tap water, and street food prepared with tap water.

reports.title: Fake 'government tourist office' near MG Road
reports.place: Near MG Road and Cubbon Park area
reports.desc: Men posing as government tourism officers approach tourists, claim their existing hotel/tour booking is invalid, and direct them to a 'government tourist office' which is actually a private shop selling overpriced packages. There's no walk-in government tourist desk on MG Road. Book tours only via karnatakatourism.org.

reports.title: SIM card scam — sold without proper KYC
reports.place: Street-side mobile shops, Majestic, Russell Market
reports.desc: Vendors sell SIM cards without proper KYC documentation. The SIM isn't registered correctly — it stops working within days or is linked to someone else's account, receiving all their calls. For foreign visitors, go directly to flagship Jio, Airtel, or BSNL stores and carry your passport + visa.

reports.title: OTP fraud call — impersonating bank officials
reports.desc: Fraudsters call posing as SBI/HDFC/ICICI officials claiming your account is 'at risk'. They request the OTP to 'block a suspicious transaction'. Once shared, the account is drained. A 21-year-old intern in Bengaluru lost ₹42,000 to this. No bank ever asks for OTP over the phone. Hang up immediately.

reports.title: Change-swap trick — auto/taxi driver swaps your ₹500 note
reports.desc: You pay ₹500 or ₹1,000 — the driver holds it, then claims you gave a lower denomination and returns the wrong change. Sleight-of-hand swap happens fast. Always use exact change or digital payment. If using cash, state the note value out loud before handing it over: 'Here is five hundred rupees.'

reports.title: Harassment and unsafe autos for women after 9 PM
reports.place: All areas, especially non-lit residential streets and outskirts
reports.desc: Women face heightened risk of verbal harassment and unsafe auto situations after 9 PM. Several cases of autos taking detours through dark roads. Australian and US Govt advisories explicitly flag Bengaluru for this. Women should use Uber/Ola (not street autos) after dark, share live location, and sit in the back seat with windows slightly down.

reports.title: Monsoon flooding — roads impassable, sinkholes appear
reports.place: Low-lying areas, Outer Ring Road, underpasses citywide
reports.desc: Bengaluru's drainage is critically underfunded — 20 minutes of rain causes severe flooding. Underpasses become death traps. Sinkholes have appeared on major roads. An estimated 93% of the city's land will be paved by end-2025, worsening runoff. During Jun–Sep rains, avoid underpasses, don't cross flooded roads, and allow 2–3× travel time.

reports.title: Children pickpockets at crowded tourist spots
reports.place: Lalbagh, Cubbon Park, Majestic Bus Station
reports.desc: Groups of children distract tourists (often dropping items near you so you bend down) while an accomplice pickpockets wallets, phones, or bags. Active near Lalbagh, Cubbon Park, and Majestic Bus Stand. Keep zipped bags in front, never put your wallet in a back pocket, and decline to help strangers who 'accidentally' drop things near you.

reports.title: Rapido / bike taxi overcharging and route manipulation
reports.desc: Bike taxi riders take longer routes than necessary or refuse to follow the app navigation, increasing the fare. Some switch off the app mid-ride and demand cash at inflated rates. Always monitor your route on your own GPS simultaneously and report any deviation through the app before paying.

reports.title: Inflated entry fees and 'special puja' upsell at temples
reports.place: ISKCON Bangalore, Bull Temple, Dodda Ganapathi Temple
reports.desc: Unofficial vendors outside and inside temples sell 'special access' or 'priority darshan' tickets at arbitrary prices (₹300–₹1,000/person). Pressure selling is aggressive, especially for foreign-looking visitors. Entry to most Bengaluru temples is free or has a clearly posted nominal fee. Don't buy from anyone outside the official temple counter.

reports.title: ATM skimming — card cloned at standalone machines
reports.place: Isolated ATMs, petrol-station ATMs, market-area standalones
reports.desc: RBI reported ATM fraud increased 22% in 2024. Skimming devices are attached to card slots and hidden cameras capture PINs. Affected users' accounts are drained before they notice. Use only ATMs inside bank branches during banking hours. Cover the keypad while entering your PIN. Enable instant SMS alerts on your card.

reports.title: Fake charity collection — staged disabled / orphan appeal
reports.place: MG Road, Brigade Road, Cubbon Park gates
reports.desc: Well-dressed individuals approach tourists with printed brochures claiming to raise money for orphans, disabled children, or disaster victims. Organisations appear legitimate but funds go to scammers. No genuine NGO solicits cash on the street in tourist zones. If you wish to donate, research registered NGOs and donate via bank transfer only.

reports.title: Auto rickshaw extreme overcharge
reports.desc: Chennai autos are notorious. ALWAYS use Uber/Ola/Rapido.

reports.title: Medical tourism middleman scam
reports.desc: Fake 'patient coordinator' offers cheap surgery at top hospital, takes advance, disappears. Book directly with hospital international patient desk.

reports.title: Marina Beach hawker overcharge
reports.desc: ₹100 corn for tourists vs ₹30 for locals. Fix price first.

reports.title: Pickpocketing in T Nagar (Pondy Bazaar)
reports.desc: Especially Saturdays. Crowded shopping district.

reports.title: Fake gold/silver shops
reports.desc: Especially Sowcarpet & Mint Street. Buy only from BIS-hallmarked retailers (Tanishq, GRT, Joyalukkas).

reports.title: Airport pre-paid taxi diversion
reports.desc: Less common than Delhi but happens.

reports.title: Auto/Taxi Meter Refusal & Inflation
reports.desc: Driver says meter is 'broken', quotes flat fare 3–10x normal. In Mumbai it's rarer because of strict meter culture. In Bangalore mostly auto drivers at airport/railway.

reports.title: Train Ticket / IRCTC Scam
reports.desc: Near railway stations, agents sell 'confirmed tickets' that are fake or for slow local trains sold as fast express. Variant: tout claims 'your train is cancelled' and herds you to a fake ticket counter to sell flights/tours at 5x rates.

reports.title: Pre-paid taxi counter at Chennai Airport
reports.desc: Government counter inside airport gives fixed rates with receipt. Avoids the touts outside. ₹400–600 to most city locations. Show receipt + driver ID match before getting in.

reports.title: Chennai Metro — clean, safe, women-friendly
reports.desc: Women-only first coach. CCTV throughout. Late-night service until 11pm. ₹10–50 fare. Way safer + faster than autos for cross-city trips.

reports.title: Pickpocketing on Kolkata Metro & at Howrah
reports.desc: Front pocket, zipped bag. Especially on Howrah-Dum Dum line during rush.

reports.title: Fake porter overcharge at Howrah
reports.desc: Real porters wear red shirt + metal armband with number. Pre-fix price: ₹50–₹100/bag.

reports.title: Kalighat 'priest' demanding offering
reports.desc: ₹500–₹5,000 demanded. Same as Varanasi pattern. Walk away firmly.

reports.title: Fake brand stores in New Market
reports.desc: Counterfeit watches, perfumes, leather.

reports.title: Auto/taxi refusing meter or quoting flat
reports.desc: Use Uber/Ola. Yellow Ambassador taxis often refuse short distances.

reports.title: Hotel switch scam
reports.desc: Less common than Delhi but happens at Howrah.

reports.title: Auto/Taxi Meter Refusal & Inflation
reports.desc: Driver says meter is 'broken', quotes flat fare 3–10x normal. In Mumbai it's rarer because of strict meter culture. In Bangalore mostly auto drivers at airport/railway.

reports.title: Train Ticket / IRCTC Scam
reports.desc: Near railway stations, agents sell 'confirmed tickets' that are fake or for slow local trains sold as fast express. Variant: tout claims 'your train is cancelled' and herds you to a fake ticket counter to sell flights/tours at 5x rates.

reports.title: Yellow Ambassador taxis run honest meters
reports.desc: The iconic yellow taxis here mostly run meters honestly — better than autos. ₹30 base + ₹15/km. Most drivers are decent and Kolkata is generally safe for solo women in daytime.

reports.title: Kolkata Metro — oldest, cheapest, safest
reports.desc: ₹5–25 fare across the city. Women-only first coach. Crowded at peak hours but safe. Quickest way to avoid Park Street traffic at evenings.

reports.title: Yellow Taxi Meter Refusal from Airport
reports.desc: Yellow Ambassador taxi drivers quote ₹800–1,200 flat for rides costing ₹300–500 on meter. Claim the meter is 'broken'. Use Ola/Uber or Yatri Sathi app. If using a yellow cab, say 'meter chharu' (start the meter). Pre-paid counter is at Arrivals between Gates 3 & 4.

reports.title: Yatri Sathi pre-payment trick at airport
reports.place: NSCBI Airport, taxi area outside Arrivals
reports.desc: The government's Yatri Sathi app replaced pre-paid taxis. Touts intercept tourists at the door and ask you to 'complete payment' on a phone before boarding — money goes to the scammer, not the driver. Payment should only be made to the driver on reaching the destination.

reports.title: Fake bag porter disappears with luggage
reports.place: Howrah Junction Railway Station, platform area
reports.desc: Unofficial porters approach tourists at the station entry, grab heavy bags, and sprint ahead into the crowd claiming to know your coach. They disappear or demand ₹500–1,000 at the coach door. Only use red-uniformed licensed porters with a numbered badge.

reports.title: Railway ticket tout — 'counter is closed'
reports.place: Outside Howrah Junction + Sealdah Station main entrance
reports.desc: Touts approach tourists claiming the ticket office is closed or trains fully booked, and offer to buy tickets from a 'government-approved' shop nearby. Hidden charges inflate the price 200–300%. Buy only on irctc.co.in or at the Tourist Quota counter inside the station.

reports.title: App-cab surge / cancel scam near Howrah
reports.place: Howrah Railway Station main exit, Sealdah forecourt
reports.desc: Drivers on Uber/Ola accept rides then call asking for cash or cancel if you refuse a surcharge above the app price. Some don't move the vehicle after acceptance, forcing cancellation fees. Screenshot the fare estimate before boarding. Report via in-app if the driver demands extra.

reports.title: Currency note swap scam
reports.place: New Market (Lindsay St), Sudder Street, Park Street vendors
reports.desc: While giving change for a ₹500 or ₹2,000 note, the vendor switches a genuine bill with a torn or fake one and claims you handed the damaged note. Happens in seconds. Use UPI/Google Pay everywhere possible. Always check every note received before pocketing.

reports.title: Fake guide at Victoria Memorial
reports.place: Victoria Memorial main entrance, Queens Way
reports.desc: Men posing as official government guides approach tourists with laminated fake ID cards. Charge ₹300–800 for tours with inaccurate history, then steer you to commission shops in Sudder Street. The official guide booth is inside the gate; guides wear ASI uniforms.

reports.title: Fake guide + forced entry at Marble Palace
reports.place: Marble Palace, Muktaram Babu Street, North Kolkata
reports.desc: Guards at the gate insist tourists can't enter without a paid guide even though it's not mandatory. Guards also block entry arbitrarily then ask for a 'tip' on exit. Photography ban used as leverage. Entry is free; bring your own knowledge or download info online.

reports.title: Fake-priest emotional trap at Kalighat Temple
reports.desc: Fake or genuine pujaris perform mantras then demand ₹2,100, ₹5,100 per item emotionally — 'give to Maa.' Each ritual triggers another demand. Total demands can reach ₹12,000–15,000. Officially, only the temple trust's counter accepts fixed donations. Refuse verbal demands mid-ritual.

reports.title: Fake donation book at Dakshineswar Temple
reports.place: Dakshineswar Kali Temple entrance + ghats near Belur Math
reports.desc: Individuals approach with printed 'donation books' and press tourists for ₹500–2,000 to a 'temple renovation fund' or 'social welfare.' Books are fake; money goes to the collector. Legitimate donations are at counters inside, only via the official UPI QR.

reports.title: Gem & handicraft investment scam
reports.place: New Market, Sudder Street, Chowringhee near Indian Museum
reports.desc: Friendly 'students' or 'locals' befriend tourists and spin a story about a family member selling gems or handicrafts at wholesale prices for resale abroad. Goods received (if any) are worthless paste gems or cheap textiles. Never buy 'wholesale export' items from a stranger.

reports.title: Overpriced tourist restaurant near Park Street
reports.desc: Touts standing outside restaurants claim to offer 'authentic Bengali' or 'rooftop special' meals and hand menus with food listed cheaply but drinks and 'service charges' tripling the bill. Check Google Maps reviews before entering. Ask for prices of water and drinks first.

reports.title: Sudder Street guesthouse bait-and-switch
reports.place: Sudder Street, New Market backpacker zone
reports.desc: Budget guesthouses quote low per-night rates online but add undisclosed cleaning fees, key deposits, or 'service tax' at check-in. Others show photos of spacious rooms but assign cramped, dirty ones. Book only on Booking.com/Agoda with a cancellation policy. Confirm final all-in price in writing before arrival.

reports.title: 31 fake hotel websites across Kolkata
reports.place: Online — Google search results, WhatsApp links
reports.desc: Fraudsters created at least 31 fake Kolkata hotel websites with cloned branding, fake reviews, and payment portals. Travellers pre-pay and arrive to find empty plots or real hotels with no record of the booking. Only book through Booking.com, Agoda, or MakeMyTrip.

reports.title: Fake tour operator websites
reports.place: Online — Google Ads targeting Kolkata tour searches
reports.desc: Clone websites mimicking legitimate Kolkata tour operators collect full booking fees via UPI or card, then disappear. Travellers arrive to no guide, no tour. Cross-check on Google Maps (look at photo history and reviews > 6 months old). Verify phone number on Truecaller before paying.

reports.title: Pickpockets at New Market (Hogg Market)
reports.desc: Crowded narrow lanes inside New Market are prime pickpocket territory. Touts 'accidentally' bump into you while an accomplice lifts your wallet or phone from a back pocket. Wear a cross-body zipped bag, keep your phone in a front pocket. Solo tourists especially targeted.

reports.title: Pickpocket on Kolkata Metro (Blue Line)
reports.place: Esplanade, Maidan, Park Street, Sealdah metro stations
reports.desc: Skilled pickpockets work in pairs on crowded metro cars during rush hour and festival days. One distracts by asking directions while the other lifts a wallet. Women have reported phone-snatching in the general compartment late at night. Travel in the women-only coach after 8 PM.

reports.title: Pickpocket in Durga Puja / festival crowds
reports.place: Pandals — Kumartuli, College Street, Lake area, Park Street
reports.desc: October Durga Puja draws massive crowds. Professional gangs operate in pandal lines where pushing is normal. Multiple bag-slashing incidents reported. Keep valuables in the hotel; carry only the cash needed for the evening. Avoid Burrabazar lanes during festival peak hours.

reports.title: Drink spiking in bars near Park Street
reports.place: Park Street nightlife strip, Esplanade-area bars
reports.desc: Travellers (especially solo) have reported drinks being spiked at lower-end bars near Park Street. Don't accept drinks from strangers and never leave your drink unattended. Stick to well-reviewed establishments on Google Maps. Leave with known companions.

reports.title: Avoid isolated ghats at night
reports.place: Hooghly riverside outside Prinsep Ghat + Babughat after 9 PM
reports.desc: Riverside ghats look romantic in pictures but the poorly-lit stretches beyond Prinsep Ghat become unsafe after dark with drug users and opportunistic crime. Only visit Prinsep Ghat and Babughat during daytime or early evening when crowds are present.

reports.title: Cycle-rickshaw blocking & harassment outside Howrah Station
reports.place: Howrah Station main exit, road toward Howrah Bridge
reports.desc: Cycle-rickshaw operators physically block tourists' path at the station exit, refusing to let them pass without hiring. Multiple operators surround you simultaneously. Book Uber/Ola before exiting or use a pre-arranged hotel cab. Don't make eye contact or stop walking.

reports.title: Avoid Esplanade after midnight
reports.place: Esplanade, Lenin Sarani, surrounding lanes after 12 AM
reports.desc: Esplanade is a busy transit point by day but after midnight becomes a hub for drug activity and aggressive touts. Female travellers especially at risk. Book Uber/Ola from inside a restaurant or hotel lobby if travelling late. Avoid walking this stretch alone at night.

reports.title: Street food contamination risk
reports.place: Sealdah, Burrabazar, market areas across Kolkata
reports.desc: Academic testing found E. coli, Salmonella, and Shigella in common Kolkata street foods including lassi, pani puri, chhatu-sarbat and sugar cane juice. 50% of samples contaminated. Stick to freshly cooked hot food; avoid water-based cold drinks from street stalls.

reports.title: Counterfeit goods sold as authentic handicrafts
reports.place: New Market, Sudder Street shops, stalls near Victoria Memorial
reports.desc: Vendors sell machine-made items as handwoven, fake 'Darjeeling' teas, and counterfeit branded goods at inflated tourist prices. Bangles and Dhokra items near Victoria Memorial are often factory-made. Buy from West Bengal government's Biswa Bangla or Manjusha emporiums for authentic goods.

reports.title: 'Friendly student' leads to commission shop
reports.place: Sudder Street, near Indian Museum, Chowringhee Road
reports.desc: Young men approach tourists claiming to be students wanting to practise English. After 10–20 minutes of genuine-feeling conversation, they suggest visiting a cousin's 'gallery' or 'textile shop.' Goods sold at massive commission. Politely decline all unsolicited shop recommendations.

reports.title: Unlicensed money changer fraud
reports.place: Near Sudder Street, Esplanade, New Market tourist zone
reports.desc: Unofficial money changers offer slightly better USD/EUR rates than banks, then short-count rupee bundles or fold fake notes inside. Never exchange currency on the street. Use SBI, authorised forex counters inside banks, or airport kiosks only.

reports.title: Unsafe tap water + ice risk
reports.place: All restaurants and street vendors across Kolkata
reports.desc: Tap water in Kolkata is unsafe to drink directly. Street food drinks use unfiltered water and unhygienic ice. Travellers regularly get severe GI illness. Drink only sealed bottled water, ask for drinks without ice unless from a verified RO system. Carry ORS sachets.

reports.title: Flooded streets during monsoon — transport collapses
reports.place: Citywide, worst in North Kolkata, Howrah, low-lying residential areas
reports.desc: Kolkata floods severely during Jun–Sep monsoon. Entire streets become knee-deep overnight. Uber/Ola cancel en masse; Metro gets suspended. Travellers stranded for hours. If visiting in monsoon, keep a 2-hour buffer for any journey and have a hotel backup.

reports.title: Fake cyber-helpdesk scam (Salt Lake Sector V)
reports.place: Online — also physical scam call centres in Salt Lake Sector V
reports.desc: Organised scam call centres in Kolkata's Sector V impersonate Amazon, Microsoft, tax office or banks targeting tourists and NRIs. They remotely access devices claiming a 'security issue.' Indian Police arrested 53 scammers from 5 call centres here in 2024. Never allow remote device access to unknown callers.

reports.title: Solo women: avoid late-night unlit lanes
reports.place: Burrabazar, Park Circus outskirts, Metiabruz after 9 PM
reports.desc: GeoSure rates Kolkata 43/100 on safety, citing women's safety as a primary concern. Female solo travellers have reported harassment in isolated lanes and empty bus stops at night. Share live trip tracking via Uber/Yatri Sathi, travel to well-lit populated areas, and book stays in Ballygunge or Park Street zone.

reports.title: Fake priest forced tilak/thread + 'donation'
reports.desc: Ties thread before you can refuse, demands ₹500–₹10,000. Hands in pockets, firm 'no'.

reports.title: Photography extortion at Manikarnika (cremation ghat)
reports.desc: Photo strictly forbidden anyway, but fake 'caretakers' threaten heavy fines if you have a camera. Don't take photos there at all — out of respect & safety.

reports.title: Boat overcharge for sunrise / Aarti
reports.desc: Quoted ₹3,000–₹5,000; real rate is ₹500–₹800 for a shared boat, ₹1,500–₹2,500 private.

reports.title: Fake guide claiming to be 'temple authority'
reports.desc: At Kashi Vishwanath, asks for 'special darshan fee' = pocket money.

reports.title: Drugged tea / lassi from random shops
reports.desc: Bhang lassi shops sometimes much stronger than declared. Tourists hospitalised.

reports.title: Hotel switch from station
reports.desc: Cyclo/auto driver claims hotel closed → commission guesthouse.

reports.title: Shoe theft at temples
reports.desc: Wear cheap sliders. Use the official locker.

reports.title: Hotel Switch / 'Your hotel burned down'
reports.desc: Taxi/auto driver claims your booked hotel is closed, burned, overbooked, or in a 'riot zone'. Drives you to a partner hotel paying 30–50% commission. Currently most active at IGI Airport T3 (after midnight arrivals) and New Delhi Railway Station.

reports.title: Stay near Assi Ghat, not Dashashwamedh
reports.desc: Quieter, more solo-women friendly, less hustle. Walk north to main ghats during day. Multiple verified women-friendly guesthouses. Avoid alleys after 10pm — they're confusing.

reports.title: Airport taxi overcharge on arrival
reports.place: Lal Bahadur Shastri Airport, arrivals exit
reports.desc: Quoted ₹2,000–₹2,500 for a ride worth ₹800–₹950. Touts swarm you the moment you exit. Use the prepaid counter inside the terminal or pre-book Ola/Uber. Sedan to ghats = ₹800–₹950 fixed.

reports.title: "Your hotel is closed/burned down" taxi lie
reports.desc: Driver insists your booked hotel is flooded, shut, or changed and takes you to a commission hotel instead. Your hotel is NOT closed. Call ahead to verify and book Ola from the station.

reports.title: Boat ride mid-river hostage
reports.desc: Quoted ₹200–₹300 per person for a sunrise boat ride. Boatman stops mid-river and demands ₹2,000–₹5,000 to "complete the tour," refusing to return until paid. Fair private boat = ₹1,200–₹2,000 total for a group. Agree on TOTAL fare before boarding — not per person, not per hour.

reports.title: Manikarnika wood donation gang
reports.desc: Men claim to be volunteers helping poor families cremate their dead. Pressure you to donate ₹300+ per kg of wood. It's a gang — money never reaches any family. Anyone can stand and watch freely. Politely walk away.

reports.title: "No photography license" fake fine at cremation ghat
reports.desc: Man approaches claiming you need a paid license to take photos and threatens police. There is no such license. Don't pay. Walk away. (Photography here is disrespectful regardless.)

reports.title: Fake Kashi Vishwanath darshan website
reports.desc: Fake sites like kashivishwanath.in charge for Sugam Darshan, Rudrabhishek and Aarti bookings — especially during Sawan when the official site suspends bookings. ₹10 lakh extracted from hundreds of devotees. Book ONLY via shrikashivishwanath.org.

reports.title: Locker shop prasad pressure near KV Temple
reports.place: Vishwanath Gali (lane to Kashi Vishwanath Temple)
reports.desc: Shops offer to store your phone and shoes before temple entry. Once stored, they pressure you to buy overpriced prasad (₹150+ per basket vs ₹20 actual). Free lockers are available INSIDE the temple corridor, provided by the KV Temple Trust.

reports.title: Fake sadhu tilak then aggressive donation demand
reports.desc: Man in saffron puts a tilak on your forehead without asking, then demands ₹500–₹2,000 — and becomes aggressive or "curses" you if you refuse. Genuine tilak donations are ₹10–₹20 and always agreed first. Don't engage unsolicited "priests."

reports.title: Fake blessing scam (name your family, then pay)
reports.desc: "Holy man" asks your parents' and siblings' names, blesses them for career / marriage luck, then demands ₹5,000–₹20,000 for the ritual. They are not priests. Any demand made AFTER a ritual they initiated = scam. Walk away.

reports.title: Station auto overcharge to ghats
reports.desc: Shared auto to Godowlia Chowk (near Dashashwamedh Ghat) should cost ₹20/seat shared or ₹80–₹120 solo. Touts quote ₹300–₹500. Walk to the prepaid auto counter inside the station or use Ola. Don't negotiate near the platform gates.

reports.title: Silk shop commission trap via auto driver
reports.desc: Driver "helpfully" takes you to his "brother's government-approved silk shop." Prices are 3–5× market rate; driver earns 20–40% commission. Never buy silk from driver-recommended shops. Go to Thatheri Bazaar or UP Handicrafts Bazaar with fixed prices.

reports.title: Machine-made silk sold as Banarasi handwoven
reports.desc: Shops label synthetic or power-loom fabric as "pure handwoven Banarasi silk" at ₹3,000–₹10,000+. Ask to see the loom certificate or GI tag. Genuine handwoven silk has visible imperfections and a GI-tagged label. When in doubt, skip.

reports.title: "Lapka" illegal guide shopping detour
reports.place: Near Dashashwamedh Ghat & old city lanes
reports.desc: Unofficial guide (locally called "lapka") offers free or cheap tours, gains trust, then steers you into commission shops where everything is overpriced. Genuine licensed guides carry a government photo ID card. If they can't show it — walk.

reports.title: Ganga Aarti "VIP seat" ticket scam
reports.desc: Touts sell "reserved VIP seats" for the Ganga Aarti at ₹200–₹500. No such ticketed seating system exists — the aarti is free and public. Arrive 30–45 min early for a good spot. Paid "VIP" tickets are worthless paper.

reports.title: "Kashi Vishwanath is closed today" redirect scam
reports.desc: Auto driver or stranger claims the temple is closed for special puja and offers to take you elsewhere — usually a silk shop or lesser temple. KV Temple is NEVER fully closed. Ignore and walk directly to the gate yourself.

reports.title: Boat tour "guide" wood donation handoff
reports.desc: Your boatman stops near the burning ghat and "helpfully" introduces you to a local guide who immediately launches into the wood donation script. It's coordinated — the boatman earns commission from the gang. Simply say "we'll watch from the water" and stay on the boat.

reports.title: "Complimentary" lassi with undisclosed bhang
reports.place: Ghat-side stalls, especially near Assi Ghat
reports.desc: Stranger or shopkeeper offers a free or cheap "special lassi." You don't know the strength. Tourists have been incapacitated for hours in unfamiliar alleys. Only consume bhang from licensed shops (Blue Lassi, Badal Thandai) with full knowledge and sober company.

reports.title: Fake travel office near station
reports.desc: Shops pose as "Government Tourist Information Centre" selling overpriced tour packages, fake tickets, and pushy itineraries. The official UP Tourism counter is INSIDE the station. Never buy from offices on the road outside the station.

reports.title: Hotel booking fraud during peak festivals
reports.desc: You book via a legitimate platform; the hotel then contacts you to pay the full amount directly to a bank account to "confirm." You arrive to find no hotel at the address. Never pay hotels outside the booking platform. Legitimate properties never request direct bank transfers.

reports.title: Pickpockets in Ganga Aarti crowd
reports.desc: The Aarti crowd is one of the densest in India. Organised pickpockets work in pairs — one distracts, one lifts. Keep phones and wallets in zipped inner pockets or money belts. Don't hold your phone out for photos without a firm grip.

reports.title: Monkey theft from hotel rooms
reports.desc: Varanasi monkeys break into hotel rooms through open windows/balconies and steal phones, food, and bags. Several hotels have wire mesh on terraces. Keep windows and doors locked even when you're inside. Don't leave food on balconies.

reports.title: Fake orphanage / school donation collection
reports.desc: Children or adults with printed letters or receipts ask for donations for schools or orphanages. Most are not affiliated with any real institution. If you want to donate, use verified NGOs (Goonj, Akshaya Patra). Never give cash to individuals on the street.

reports.title: Inflated photo charge at Sarnath
reports.desc: Unofficial "guards" demand ₹100–₹500 for the right to take photos at the stupa. The Archaeological Survey of India combo ticket (₹20 Indian / ₹200 foreigner) covers photography. Never pay a person on the ground — only at the official ASI counter.
