reports.title: Overpriced street food "foreigner menu"
reports.desc: Some stalls show foreigners a separate, higher-priced menu. Kachori sabzi should cost ₹20–₹30; chai ₹10–₹15. If a price feels wrong, walk to the next stall. Locals' spots 1–2 lanes away from the main ghat always have fair prices.

reports.title: Shared boat "per person" vs "per boat" price switch
reports.desc: Boatman quotes what sounds like a total, then at the journey's end demands that price per person. Always confirm in writing (screenshot or note) that the amount quoted is the TOTAL for your group — not per person, not per ghat stop. Settle this before you step in.

reports.title: Aggressive "photography fee" at Manikarnika viewpoint
reports.desc: Men lead tourists to a "viewpoint" terrace and then demand ₹200–₹1,000 for having watched the cremation from there. Photography at Manikarnika is not permitted as a paid service and is considered deeply disrespectful. Don't follow strangers to any "viewpoint" here.

reports.title: ATM skimming near busy ghat areas
reports.place: ATMs in tourist-heavy Old City lanes
reports.desc: Tampered ATMs near the ghats can clone your card. Use ATMs inside bank branches only — the Cantt and Lanka/BHU areas are safer. Cover your PIN. Prefer UPI, or carry cash drawn from your home-city bank where possible.

reports.title: Prasad sellers blocking temple corridor exits
reports.desc: Vendors block the narrow corridor exits selling prasad baskets at ₹150+ per person, creating pressure to buy. Purchasing is entirely optional. Official temple-trust prasad counters are inside and priced clearly. You can exit without buying anything from corridor vendors.

reports.title: Night taxi surge scam from airport
reports.place: Lal Bahadur Shastri Airport, after 9 PM
reports.desc: Late-night arrivals face severely inflated quotes (₹2,500–₹3,500) with no prepaid counter staff on duty. Pre-book your cab before you land. The official night rate for a sedan is ₹900–₹1,100 including a ₹300 driver allowance after 9 PM — not ₹2,500+.

reports.title: Fake "lost tourist" distraction for pickpocket
reports.desc: A well-dressed person approaches asking for directions or help reading a map — a second person lifts your bag, phone, or pocket contents while you're distracted. Works in pairs. Keep bags on your front, phones pocketed, and don't assist strangers who approach with items in hand.

reports.title: Boat ride overcharge on Lake Pichola
reports.desc: Official rates posted at City Palace ticket counter. Ignore touts at the ghat.

reports.title: Gem & miniature painting export scam
reports.desc: Same as Jaipur. 'Family workshop' = commission. Walk away.

reports.title: Fake heritage 'haveli' bookings
reports.desc: Several scam websites in 2025 — book via Booking.com/MMT only.

reports.title: Auto refusing meter from station
reports.desc: Use Uber/Ola — Udaipur has good coverage now.

reports.title: Fake guide at City Palace
reports.desc: Hire only from official counter.

reports.title: Sunset Point camel/horse ride overcharge
reports.desc: Fix price before mounting.

reports.title: Gem & Carpet Export Scam
reports.desc: Friendly local introduces you to a 'family jeweller'. You're told you can 'export' gems duty-free and resell abroad for huge profit; they ship to your country. Stones are worthless glass/synthetic. Variant: a fake government 'official' calls later demanding fines.

reports.title: Fake Priest / Tilak / Forced Blessing
reports.desc: A 'pandit' ties a thread (kalava) on your wrist or applies tilak before you can refuse, then chants a 'blessing' and demands ₹500–₹10,000 'donation'. Most aggressive at Manikarnika and Dashashwamedh ghats in Varanasi.

reports.title: Udaipur is the safest big tourist city in India
reports.desc: Consistently rated safest big tourist city for solo women. Walking the Old City alone after dark is fine. Locals genuinely helpful, not pushy. Stay near Lake Pichola for best experience.

reports.title: Drug possession setup (Old Manali / Kasol nearby)
reports.desc: Charas offered → 'cop' demands bribe. NDPS Act jail risk. Refuse all offers.

reports.title: Bike/scooter rental damage scam
reports.desc: Photo before+after, never leave passport, use known agencies.

reports.title: Taxi cartel rates to Rohtang/Solang
reports.desc: Fixed by union; high but unavoidable. Hotel can sometimes negotiate.

reports.title: Paragliding operator overcharge / no insurance
reports.desc: Use only HP Tourism-licensed operators. Verify pilot license.

reports.title: Honeymoon package fraud online
reports.desc: Fake Instagram/WhatsApp packages with advance UPI demand. Lose ₹20K–₹1L.

reports.title: Photographer at Hidimba Temple / Mall Road
reports.desc: 'Free photo with traditional dress' → demands ₹500–₹2,000.

reports.title: Drug Possession Setup (Goa)
reports.desc: Local offers drugs at a beach/party; once accepted/handed over, accomplice 'cop' arrives, demands large bribe to avoid arrest. Punishment for narcotics in India is severe — even small amounts = years in jail.

reports.title: Old Manali is the solo-traveller hub
reports.place: Across the river from Mall Road
reports.desc: Cafe culture, hostels with solo women travellers, walking-friendly. Way safer than the touristy Mall Road. Stay at Hosteller / Zostel / Madpackers — all women-friendly chains.

reports.title: Stalking by local posing as friendly 'guide'
reports.desc: A local man on a bike offered hotel referrals, then repeatedly called a solo woman traveller with unsolicited massage offers, marijuana comments, and remarks about her body. Tracked her to Old Manali market saying 'Aapke figure se pehchan liya'. Blocked calls only after threatening police. Report to Manali Police: 01902-252310.

reports.title: Fake snow 'ice point' at Solang Valley
reports.place: Solang Valley, 14 km from Manali town
reports.desc: Tourists charged ski rental and activity fees and taken to a narrow artificial strip of imported snow on bare rocky terrain — no natural snowfall present. Viral video by Instagram user @atulchauhan1512 (Dec 2025) showed tourists in ski suits on fake snow. Always check live snow conditions at rohtangpermits.hp.gov.in before paying.

reports.title: Fake hotel websites stealing advance payments
reports.place: Online — targets tourists booking before arrival
reports.desc: Cybercriminals copy photos from real Manali hotels and build duplicate websites. Tourists pay advance, arrive to find no booking. Multiple FIRs filed. Manali Hoteliers Association has flagged this repeatedly. Only book via Booking.com, MakeMyTrip, or call hotel directly on a verified number from Google Maps.

reports.title: Travel agent bait-and-switch hotel package
reports.place: Booked from Delhi/other cities, discovered on arrival
reports.desc: Tourists book 'deluxe' packages at heavy discount. On arrival placed in cheap, dirty hotels near Mall Road. Agents stop responding after payment. Example: Yaarana Holiday Tours — paid Rs 3,000 token, hotel confirmation switched 1 day before travel with demand of Rs 15,000 extra. No refund for 30+ days.

reports.title: Taxi overcharge from Manali ISBT bus stand
reports.desc: Arriving tourists — especially solo women — are surrounded by taxi touts claiming hotels are 'far', 'no buses available', and quoting 3–4× actual rates. Actual local fare for Mall Road / Old Manali drops: Rs 100–200. Touts quote Rs 500–800. Use HRTC local buses or agree fixed rate BEFORE getting in. No Ola/Uber in Manali.

reports.title: Adventure activity ghost booking — paragliding
reports.desc: Fraudulent operators take full paragliding or rafting payment upfront online or via WhatsApp, then disappear or don't show up. Tripadvisor review: 'No one there to do the ride! It is FAKE!'. Official govt rates: Paragliding Rs 3,800 + Rs 500 video. Book only through HPTDC or operators with physical counters at Solang Valley.

reports.title: Equipment damage scam — ski / bike rental
reports.place: Solang Valley rental shops + Old Manali bike rentals
reports.desc: Rental vendors charge tourists for pre-existing damage when equipment is returned. Common with skis, snowboards and Royal Enfield bike rentals (Rs 500–1,000/day). Vendor claims a fresh scratch is your fault and demands Rs 3,000–10,000. Always photograph the equipment all-around BEFORE riding away, and ensure the vendor signs off.

reports.title: Drug sell-then-police-tip-off trap
reports.place: Old Manali village lanes & alleys behind Mall Road
reports.desc: Sellers approach tourists offering charas/weed. After sale, the seller tips off local police. Tourist is caught and extorted for Rs 10,000–50,000 bribe to avoid arrest. Some get sold fake substances instead. Possession of any illegal narcotic is criminal under NDPS — severe jail sentences apply. Avoid any unsolicited drug offer.

reports.title: Unwanted contact from touts after overnight bus arrival
reports.place: Manali ISBT Bus Stand — 3 to 5 AM arrivals
reports.desc: Solo women arriving at 3–5 AM on overnight buses from Delhi are primary targets. Men offering 'cheap hotel rooms' on bikes follow women from the bus stand. One documented case: woman followed from bus stand to Old Manali, given a room, then received repeated calls from the 'helpful' man. Pre-book accommodation and arrange hotel pickup before travel.

reports.title: Fake Rohtang permit agents overcharging
reports.place: Near Manali SDM office and travel agent shops
reports.desc: Agents charge Rs 1,500–3,000 for Rohtang permits that officially cost Rs 550 (Rs 500 permit + Rs 50 congestion fee) for cars. Permits available free to apply at rohtangpermits.hp.gov.in or the official app. Daily limit is 1,200 vehicles. Tuesdays: road closed for maintenance, no permits issued. Don't pay anyone more than Rs 550.

reports.title: Massage parlour harassment of solo women
reports.desc: Solo women travellers are approached with offers of 'Ayurvedic massage for weight loss' or 'body treatments' by men — often the same who initially posed as helpful guides. These aren't registered spas. Refusal is often followed by persistence, follow-up calls, and body-shaming comments. Block all numbers immediately. Tourist police helpline: 1800-180-8008.

reports.title: Monsoon road travel — NH-3 landslide danger
reports.desc: Jul–Aug 2025: NH-3 blocked multiple times at Pandoh Dam area. 173 deaths across HP in monsoon 2025. Boulders hit moving vehicles — one tourist from MP died near tunnel no. 2, a woman critically injured at the same spot Jul 2025. Don't travel Chandigarh-Manali highway Jul–Aug. Check HRTC road status before departing.

reports.title: Flash floods trap tourists — 2,500+ vehicles stranded
reports.place: Manali town and Beas River banks
reports.desc: August 2025 monsoon floods collapsed sections of NH-3 connecting Manali to Leh. 2,500+ vehicles stranded. Hotels and restaurants along Beas River washed away. Mobile and internet connectivity snapped for days. Avoid Manali entirely Jul to mid-Sep. If travelling, keep 3–4 days of emergency supplies and offline maps downloaded.

reports.title: 'Lucky draw' / 'special discount' cash scam
reports.place: Mall Road and Old Manali market shops
reports.desc: Tourists told they have 'won a lucky draw' or given a special discount on handicrafts, pashmina, or adventure packages — but must pay cash immediately. Items overpriced by 300–500% and quality is fake (synthetic 'pashmina', low-grade wood carvings). Compare prices across 3 shops. Shop at Bhuttico Weavers (govt-certified) for authentic Himachali products.

reports.title: Uninvited male company during solo night walks on Mall Road
reports.place: Mall Road and dark alleys behind Mall Road
reports.desc: Solo women walking Mall Road after 9 PM are frequently approached by men offering company, directions, or 'local knowledge'. Behind Mall Road alleys, drug users and dealers are active. Police confirmed: 'Crooks catch you in dark alleys and try to sell cocaine and hash.' Women should walk Mall Road only before 9 PM and always with at least one other person after dark.

reports.title: Private luxury bus — false pickup / drop promises
reports.desc: Private bus operators take full payment, quote specific pickup/drop points, then change timing by hours or drop at non-standard locations. Buses also charge Rs 200–500 extra for luggage beyond 15 kg. Some reach Manali at 3 AM instead of announced 6–7 AM, leaving solo women stranded at the dark bus stand. Use HRTC government Volvo (book at hrtchp.com).

reports.title: Altitude sickness ignored by tour operators at Rohtang
reports.place: Rohtang Pass (3,978 m) and high-altitude tour routes
reports.desc: Tour operators rush tourists to Rohtang Pass same-day as Manali arrival without acclimatisation. Symptoms: headache, vomiting, dizziness, breathlessness. In severe cases HACE or HAPE can be fatal. Operators downplay risk. Spend minimum 1–2 days in Manali (2,050 m) before going higher. Carry Diamox if prescribed. Rohtang is NOT suitable for heart or asthma patients.

reports.title: Forced photo-taking by strangers without consent
reports.place: Hidimba Temple, Rohtang Pass, Solang Valley
reports.desc: Solo and group women travellers report being photographed without consent by other male tourists — especially at crowded spots like Hidimba Temple and Rohtang. Men ask for 'just one selfie' and use refusal as a pretext for aggressive behaviour. Keep tourist police number saved: 01902-252310. File a complaint if photographed without consent.

reports.title: River rafting by non-licensed operators — safety risk
reports.place: Beas River, Pirdi to Jhiri stretch (Kullu side)
reports.desc: Unlicensed rafting operators tout on Mall Road with below-market prices (Rs 399–500 per person vs official Rs 1,500). These operators use outdated equipment without valid safety certification. At least one drowning incident near Pirdi in the 2024 season. Book rafting only through HPTDC-registered operators or hotels. Govt rate: Rs 1,500 (7 km), Rs 1,800 (9 km).

reports.title: Drinking water scam — tap water sold as mineral water
reports.place: Tourist cafés and dhabas in Old Manali and Vashisht
reports.desc: Sealed-looking mineral water bottles refilled with tap water and re-sealed are sold at Rs 50–80 each. Tap water in Manali isn't always safe — can cause stomach infections and giardia. Check seal carefully — original MRP sticker should show manufacturer address. Buy from established chemist shops. Himalayan and Bisleri are generally safer.

reports.title: Chitta (heroin) dealers targeting young tourists
reports.place: Manali Gram Panchayat area and surrounding villages
reports.desc: Chitta (synthetic heroin) dealers — including organised inter-state smuggling networks — are active around Manali village and approach tourists, especially youth. Manali Gram Panchayat declared an emergency Feb 2025, formed a committee, shut all shops after 10 PM, banned DJs, and offered Rs 15,000 reward for information on smugglers. Never accept food or drink from strangers.

reports.title: Isolated trekking trail — solo women followed on descent
reports.place: Jogini Waterfall trail from Vashisht village
reports.desc: Jogini Waterfall trail (1–1.5 hr hike from Vashisht) is popular but isolated after 5 PM. No phone signal on trail. Solo women have reported being followed on the descent. Do Jogini only before 2 PM in a group or with a certified guide. Hampta Pass and Beas Kund trailheads are similarly isolated. Never begin a trek after 3 PM.

reports.title: Fake trekking 'guide' — no certification, wrong route
reports.place: Old Manali cafés and bus stand
reports.desc: Uncertified men pose as 'certified trekking guides' for Hampta Pass, Bhrigu Lake and Beas Kund treks at below-market rates (Rs 500–800/day vs Rs 1,500–2,500 official). Several groups reported being taken on wrong routes or left mid-trail. Hire only through IMF (Indian Mountaineering Foundation) certified operators or verified hotels. Always check photo ID.

reports.title: Hostel dorm 'female-only' is mixed-gender after dark
reports.place: Budget hostels in Old Manali and Vashisht
reports.desc: Several budget hostels (under Rs 500/night) advertise 'female dorms' but have broken locks, no lockers, or secretly allow mixed-gender access after dark. Female travellers report finding unknown men in dorms at night. Always inspect room lock and locker on arrival and ask: 'Is this a female-only dorm with a separate key?' Book through Booking.com and read female traveller reviews.

reports.title: Self-drive on snowy / icy roads — fatal accidents
reports.place: Rohtang Pass road, Solang Valley to Marhi stretch
reports.desc: Tourists rent self-drive cars and attempt Rohtang Pass road without ice-driving experience. Multiple fatal accidents each season. Apr 2025 advisory: 'Some portions still slippery and can prove fatal if driver has no experience.' No Ola/Uber available. Kullu DC advises hiring local experienced taxi drivers for all snowy road sections. Snow chains mandatory in winter.

reports.title: OYO-listed hotel — room not as shown, hidden charges
reports.place: Budget hotel areas near Mall Road
reports.desc: OYO-listed hotels show 4-star photos but deliver dirty, cold, poorly maintained rooms. Extra charges for heater in winter (Rs 200–500/night) not disclosed upfront. Early check-in before 11 AM charged without disclosure. Always record a full room video on arrival and demand a written list of all charges before accepting the room.

reports.title: Fake 'Malana Cream' — tourist sold brick dust as charas
reports.place: Old Manali lanes; dealers claiming 'Malana product'
reports.desc: Sellers offer 'premium Malana Cream' charas at Rs 5,000–20,000 per tola. Many tourists receive fake product — compressed brick dust, henna, or low-grade material. Apart from being cheated financially, any possession of real or fake charas is a criminal NDPS offence. Tourists have been murdered by local drug gangs in the valley. Stay completely away.

reports.title: Solang Valley activity bundle overcharge
reports.place: Solang Valley activity counters, 14 km from Manali
reports.desc: Official activity bundle (Ziplining + Skiing + Yak Ride + Tube Slide + Snow dress) is government-priced at Rs 3,000. Touts charge Rs 4,500–6,000 for the same package, adding fake 'government tax', 'insurance fee', 'season surcharge'. Always go to counters inside the activity zone — not touts at the road entrance. Compare with HPTDC official rates.

reports.title: Night bus arrival at 3 AM — solo women left at dark stand
reports.desc: Overnight Volvo buses frequently arrive at Manali 2–3 AM instead of promised 6–7 AM. Women left at a poorly-lit bus stand surrounded by touts and drug users. No auto/cab on meter at that hour. Multiple cases of men following women from the bus. Pre-arrange hotel pickup — most hotels will send a driver for Rs 100–200 extra. Share live location with family throughout the journey.

reports.title: Digital payment spoofing — fake UPI 'success' screens
reports.place: Mall Road shops and smaller markets
reports.desc: Shopkeepers use apps that generate fake UPI payment success screens or confirmation sounds after you scan their QR. Money is not actually transferred but they claim payment received. Common in smaller shops and temporary stalls. Always check YOUR bank's SMS confirmation or app before leaving the counter. Never accept a seller's screenshot as proof of payment.

reports.title: Fake houseboat / backwater booking online
reports.desc: Lookalike Kerala Tourism websites. Pay advance, no boat. Book via keralatourism.org or trusted operator (Spice Coast, Lakes & Lagoons).

reports.title: Auto/tuk-tuk overcharge in Fort Kochi
reports.desc: Use Uber/Ola/Rapido — strong coverage.

reports.title: Fake antique / spice 'discount' shops in Jew Town
reports.desc: Inflated prices for 'genuine' spices and 'antiques'. Bargain hard or buy from chain stores.

reports.title: Chinese fishing net 'demonstration fee'
reports.desc: Operators ask ₹500–₹1,000 to 'pull the net'. Fixed local rate is ₹100–₹200.

reports.title: Cruise booking scam (Cordelia)
reports.desc: Fake websites. Book only via cordeliacruises.com or authorised agents.

reports.title: Fake Ayurveda spa packages
reports.desc: Some 'Ayurvedic clinics' have no licensed doctors. Check NABH-accreditation.

reports.title: Beach Shack & Water Sports Overcharge
reports.desc: Menus without prices, jet ski/parasail operators charging 5–10x and damaging equipment to demand 'damage' fees. Renting bikes/scooters with fake damage claims.

reports.title: Cordelia / Cruise Booking Scam
reports.desc: Fake Cordelia / Costa websites take advance for cruises that never sail. Surged in 2025 per Goa MP's statement in Rajya Sabha.

reports.title: Kochi Metro + ferry combo for safe travel
reports.desc: Metro covers Ernakulam side. Public ferry to Fort Kochi from High Court Junction is ₹4 + safe + scenic. Avoids touristy auto overcharging by 5–10x.

reports.title: Fort Kochi homestays are safest accommodation
reports.desc: Verified women-friendly homestays in colonial heritage area. Owner-run, walking distance to all sights, ₹1,500–3,500/night. Way better than the mainland hotels for solo women.

reports.title: Man harasses Taiwanese tourist during Kochi Biennale boat ride
reports.place: Pepper House, Fort Kochi (Biennale venue) and backwaters boat
reports.desc: A Taiwanese woman was befriended by a Mumbai man outside Pepper House on Jan 18, 2026. The next day during a boat ride he made unwanted physical contact and forced her into an auto. Fort Kochi police registered a case. Solo women at large events should be extremely cautious of strangers who befriend them at venues.

reports.title: Fort Kochi streets go dark and deserted by 7:30 PM
reports.place: Fort Kochi streets, near Chinese Fishing Nets and Bazaar Road
reports.desc: Multiple solo female travellers and local sources confirm Fort Kochi's streets become largely deserted by 7–7:30 PM. Dimly-lit alleys near the waterfront have been flagged in safety reports including a 2025 incident involving a British backpacker. Women should plan to be back at accommodation by 7 PM or travel in groups after dark.

reports.title: British backpacker sexually assaulted near Chinese Fishing Nets at night
reports.place: Waterfront alley near Chinese Fishing Nets, Fort Kochi
reports.desc: A 28-year-old British backpacker reported a sexual assault during a late-night walk along the Fort Kochi waterfront on November 18, 2025. She was attacked by two men in a dimly-lit alley. One suspect (a local fisherman with prior theft charges) was arrested; the second was being sought. CCTV footage confirmed the attack. Avoid this area alone at night.

reports.title: Verbal harassment of women common after sunset on Marine Drive
reports.desc: Marine Drive is a popular tourist evening promenade but safety data (WorldTravelIndex 2024) shows only 59/100 residents and visitors feel safe at night in Kochi. Multiple solo women travellers report uncomfortable staring, following, and verbal comments especially after 8 PM. Walk in groups, stay in well-lit sections near shops, and avoid isolated benches.

reports.title: Kerala recorded 25% spike in violent crimes against tourists in 2025
reports.desc: Kerala Tourism Department data shows a 25% spike in reported violent crimes against tourists in the first 9 months of 2025, with sexual offences accounting for nearly 40% of incidents. Solo female travellers are disproportionately affected. Kerala police have deployed women-only patrol vans but solo women should use the official Abhaya helpline (1091) and stay in registered homestays.

reports.title: Actress stalked and physically assaulted in a Kochi shopping mall
reports.desc: Kerala Women's Commission registered a suo motu case after a Malayalam actress reported being stalked and physically assaulted by two men in a Kochi mall — one grazed her while walking close. Kerala Police also took cognizance. Even crowded public spaces like malls aren't fully safe — be alert and move to staffed/security areas immediately if followed.

reports.title: Airport prepaid taxi 'upgrades' you to expensive Innova without consent
reports.place: Cochin International Airport (COK), Nedumbassery — prepaid counter
reports.desc: Traveller arrived at Kochi airport and requested a small sedan (Etios/Swift) to Thrissur. The counter agent issued an Innova receipt costing ₹2,079 versus the ~₹1,200 Uber price, claiming vehicle allocation is 'random'. Insist on the specific vehicle class you request. If refused, use Uber/Ola from the road outside the airport terminal boundary.

reports.title: Unauthorised touts outside airport grab luggage and demand inflated fares
reports.place: Cochin International Airport (COK) arrivals exit — outside terminal
reports.desc: Unofficial 'helpers' in the arrivals zone approach tired passengers, grab bags, and steer them toward private unlicensed cars quoting tourist prices (2–3× standard fare). Always use the official prepaid taxi counters INSIDE the terminal. The counter for Kochi city charges ~₹700–₹1,000. Don't hand luggage to anyone not in uniform.

reports.title: Autorickshaws refuse meter and charge 3× tourist price
reports.place: Fort Kochi, Mattancherry, MG Road tourist areas
reports.desc: Auto drivers in Fort Kochi regularly refuse the meter, especially for foreigners, quoting lump-sum fares 2–3× the metered rate. Common excuses: 'meter broken' or 'no return ride'. Metered fare from Fort Kochi to Ernakulam should be ~₹120–160. Always insist on meter use. If refused, use Ola/Uber Auto which shows fixed fare before booking.

reports.title: Auto driver takes long route claiming it's the 'only road'
reports.place: Ernakulam town, near Broadway Market and Ernakulam Junction
reports.desc: Tourist auto drivers frequently take significantly longer routes, especially to Fort Kochi and Mattancherry, to inflate metered fares. Open Google Maps and track in real time before getting in. If the driver deviates significantly, ask them to correct the route immediately. Note the auto plate for complaints to RTO at 1800-425-1550.

reports.title: Drivers ask for your used Indian SIM at airport — don't give it
reports.place: Kochi and surrounding areas, especially airport-bound rides
reports.desc: Drivers taking tourists to the airport ask passengers for their old SIM 'since you won't need it'. The driver sells it (with remaining credit) at airport kiosks to the next tourists — who then receive calls/SMSes meant for someone else and face fraud risks. Destroy or keep your Indian SIM. Don't give it to any driver.

reports.title: Tourist spice shops near Fort Kochi charge 2–4× Broadway market prices
reports.place: Spice Bazaar Road, Fort Kochi / Mattancherry
reports.desc: Multiple tourists report spice shops near Fort Kochi's tourist zone charge massively inflated prices. A 250g banana chips packet sold for ₹250 that costs ₹125 outside. One shop swapped a smaller cinnamon packet for a larger expensive one in the buyer's basket aggressively. Buy spices from Broadway Market, Ernakulam (wholesale lane) or the Spices Board outlet at Palarivattom — 40–60% cheaper.

reports.title: Antique shops in Mattancherry sell fake 'antiques'
reports.desc: Shops lining Mattancherry's Jew Town prominently market items as 'antiques' but Tripadvisor long-term community reviews and local residents confirm most items are new replicas. Sellers use age-old decor and dust to fake antiquity. Don't pay antique prices for any item. Bargain aggressively or skip entirely. If serious about antiques, take a knowledgeable local guide.

reports.title: Fake 'official' guides approach tourists at Chinese Fishing Nets
reports.place: Chinese Fishing Nets area, Fort Kochi
reports.desc: Individuals without credentials approach tourists at the Chinese Fishing Nets and Mattancherry Palace claiming to be official government-licensed guides. They provide poor information and demand ₹500–₹2,000. Real licensed guides wear KTDC ID cards. Verify ID before hiring. Book guides only through KTDC office or your hotel. Politely ignore street approaches.

reports.title: Fake hotel booking websites clone real Kerala properties
reports.place: Online — Google search ads targeting Kochi hotels
reports.desc: Cybercrime units confirmed fraudsters create professional-looking fake websites cloning actual Kerala hotels. Tourists pay UPI/bank advance and discover on arrival no real booking exists. Affected mainly during peak season (Dec–March). Always book through official hotel websites, Booking.com, or MakeMyTrip. Never pay via WhatsApp or personal UPI links sent by 'hotel staff'.

reports.title: Auto driver insists 'sightseeing tour' includes commission spice shop
reports.place: Fort Kochi and Mattancherry tourist circuit
reports.desc: Several Tripadvisor reviewers noted that auto drivers offering guided Kochi tours insist on including spice/souvenir shops where they receive commission. Drivers say shops are 'government authorised' which is false. You'll be pressured to buy. Tell the driver at the start: 'no shops, only the places I tell you'. Use Ola/Uber for point-to-point to avoid this entirely.

reports.title: Fake Ayurvedic massage centres charge ₹3,600 for a basic body massage
reports.place: Various massage parlours in Fort Kochi tourist area
reports.desc: Tourists seeking authentic Ayurvedic massage are lured into parlours claiming traditional treatments. Common complaints: non-Ayurvedic oils used, poorly-trained staff, dark cramped rooms, prices of ₹2,500–₹4,000 for services worth ₹600–₹800. Hotel touts also send tourists to these for commissions. Book Ayurvedic massage only at NABH-accredited hospitals or reputed wellness centres.

reports.title: Houseboat operators add undisclosed charges after boarding
reports.place: Vembanad Lake / Kumbalangi backwater entry points
reports.desc: Tourists booking budget houseboats report additional charges demanded on board for items listed as 'included' — fuel surcharge, AC charge, cook's tip, fishing-net viewing. These can add ₹1,500–₹3,000 to the agreed price. Always get a signed written itinerary with all charges BEFORE boarding. Only use Kerala Maritime Board licensed operators (check the licence displayed on the boat).

reports.title: Restaurants near Chinese Fishing Nets overcharge with no menu
reports.place: Restaurants and seafood shacks at Chinese Fishing Nets beach
reports.desc: Multiple tourists report being charged 3–5× normal prices at casual eateries near the Chinese Fishing Nets, especially for fresh-catch seafood. Pricing per kg is quoted verbally after cooking (you can't refuse). Always ask for a written price list BEFORE ordering. Avoid any place without a visible menu board. Meal for two shouldn't exceed ₹600–₹900 at local eateries.

reports.title: Locals in Kathakali makeup charge for unsolicited photos
reports.place: Fort Kochi streets near Kathakali performance centres
reports.desc: Men dressed in Kathakali costume stand near tourist photo spots and invite tourists to pose for free. After photos are taken, they aggressively demand ₹500–₹2,000 — turning hostile if refused. Never photograph anyone in traditional costume without explicitly agreeing on price (or zero price) first. Ignore invitations from costumed individuals at tourist sites.

reports.title: Fake Kochi Metro ticket touts sell ₹10 tokens for ₹50
reports.place: Kochi Metro — Ernakulam South & MG Road station exits
reports.desc: Individuals near metro entrances approach tourists and offer to sell metro tokens, claiming the inside queue is 'very long'. They charge ₹50–₹100 for ₹10–₹20 tokens. Metro tokens must be bought from official counters or the Kochi1 app. Don't buy from street sellers outside the station. Queues inside are typically short.

reports.title: Private boats charge ₹500 for the ₹5 government ferry crossing
reports.place: Fort Kochi – Ernakulam water route, High Court & Customs jetties
reports.desc: Private small boat operators at the Fort Kochi waterfront approach tourists and offer 'ferry' rides to Ernakulam for ₹300–₹500 per person. The official KCSWTD government ferry between Fort Kochi and Ernakulam costs just ₹4–₹6 and runs every 15–20 minutes. Use only the green government ferry boats at marked jetties. Ignore private operators.

reports.title: Gold chain snatching from women — rising incidents in Ernakulam district
reports.desc: An April 2026 Manorama Online report confirmed a chain-snatching incident in Ernakulam involving a 74-year-old woman approached under the pretext of buying something. Chain snatchers on motorbikes (two riders) snatch gold jewellery from women walking alone on roadsides. Avoid wearing visible gold jewellery on public streets. Walk away from the traffic side; hold bag close.

reports.title: Fake Booking.com hotel messages demand extra payment before arrival
reports.place: Online — targeting travellers with upcoming Kochi hotel bookings
reports.desc: Tripadvisor Kerala forum flagged a phishing scam where travellers receive official-looking Booking.com messages/emails demanding credit-card re-verification before arrival. The link is fake and captures card details. Booking.com never asks for full card re-entry by message. If you get such a message, call the hotel directly using numbers from the official Booking.com platform only.

reports.title: Women in 'Western' clothing face staring and verbal comments in Mattancherry
reports.place: Mattancherry and parts of Ernakulam market areas
reports.desc: Solo female travellers report heightened staring, catcalling and verbal comments in Mattancherry's local market zone (not the tourist area near Dutch Palace). Conservative dress (covering shoulders and knees) reduces but doesn't eliminate harassment. Avoid narrow market lanes alone. Have your accommodation's number on speed dial. Tourist police helpline: 0484-2722028.

reports.title: Women advised not to take unregistered private taxis alone at night
reports.place: Citywide — Fort Kochi, Marine Drive, station areas at night
reports.desc: Multiple safety guides for solo female travellers in Kochi advise against getting into private/unregistered vehicles alone at night. Night rides from Ernakulam Junction or after late events have had reported incidents. Always use Uber/Ola at night (tracked rides), share trip details with someone, and sit in the back seat. Screenshot the driver's name and car plate before starting.

reports.title: Unregistered jet ski operators at Cherai Beach demand ₹3,000 for short rides
reports.place: Cherai Beach, Vypin Island, north Kochi
reports.desc: Unlicensed water-sports operators at Cherai Beach quote vague prices pre-ride and then charge inflated amounts after. Reports of ₹2,500–₹3,500 for 10-minute jet ski rides (regulated price: ₹800–₹1,200). Only use operators with a visible registration board showing rates. Confirm price in writing (photo of rate board) before getting on any equipment.

reports.title: Lone women travellers targeted by 'friendly helpers' on Ernakulam Junction platform
reports.place: Ernakulam Junction railway station, platform area
reports.desc: Solo women arriving by train at Ernakulam Junction report men posing as 'tourist helpers' who offer to carry bags and guide to taxis outside. They route victims to overpriced private cars or demand ₹200–₹500 for 'help'. Ernakulam station has an official porter service with fixed rates (₹50–₹80 per bag) and a prepaid taxi counter. Decline all unsolicited helpers firmly.

reports.title: Groups of men loiter outside Fort Kochi restaurants harassing solo women at exit
reports.place: Fort Kochi restaurant lane (Princess Street / Burgher Street)
reports.desc: Female travellers report groups of men loitering near the exits of popular Fort Kochi restaurants, especially weekends and Biennale season (Dec–Mar). Verbal comments, following, and physical proximity harassment reported. Ask restaurant staff to call your auto/cab before stepping outside. One Fort Kochi restaurant was specifically noted by a solo woman traveller for staff who helped her get an auto safely.

reports.title: Kochi Biennale venues draw opportunistic harassers — extra caution Dec–March
reports.place: Biennale venues — Aspinwall House, Pepper House, Cabral Yard
reports.desc: Two major sexual harassment cases emerged from the Kochi Biennale 2025–26 season: a Taiwanese tourist harassed on a boat (Jan 2026) and a harassment complaint against a co-founder of the Biennale Foundation (Dec 2025). The event draws large crowds in confined art spaces. Women should avoid exploring indoor Biennale spaces alone, keep phone charged, and stick to groups especially at evening events.

reports.title: Avoid motorcycle taxis (bike taxis) — high accident rate and unregulated
reports.desc: India's travel-advisory data confirms motorcycle taxis in Kerala account for 34% of tourist-transport injuries. In Kochi, bike-taxi apps operate in a grey zone with unvetted drivers and no helmets provided to passengers. A solo woman on a bike taxi is also vulnerable to harassment. Stick to Uber/Ola cars, the Kochi Water Metro (highly recommended), or government buses.

reports.title: Taxi flat-rate scam from Shibuya
reports.desc: Driver offers 'flat rate' to your hotel but charges 3× the meter. Always insist on the meter or use official Tokyo taxis (JPN Taxi app).

reports.title: Rickshaw overcharging in Asakusa
reports.desc: Jinrikisha (rickshaw) riders quote 500 yen, bill ¥5,000 per person after the ride. Agree on price and duration in writing beforehand.

reports.title: Fake 'English students' near Harajuku
reports.desc: Approach claiming to be English students who need practice. Leads to expensive restaurant or bar — bill split between you and 'host'.

reports.title: Bar tout harassment in Roppongi
reports.desc: Men outside bars offer 'free drinks' — bill arrives for thousands of yen. Avoid bars with persistent touts on the street.

reports.title: Pickpocketing on Yamanote Line rush hour
reports.desc: Morning rush (7–9 AM) on the loop line is a prime window. Use women-only carriages (first carriage, pink signage) and keep bags in front.

reports.title: Fake monk donation request in Shinjuku
reports.desc: Robed men approach, hand you a charm, then demand large donation. Legitimate monks do not solicit tourists on streets.

reports.title: Tuk-tuk 'closed temple' scam near Grand Palace
reports.desc: Tuk-tuk driver says temple is closed for cleaning, offers to take you to a 'local market' — a gem store paying the driver commission. Grand Palace is almost never closed to tourists.

reports.title: Gem scam near Siam Square
reports.desc: Friendly local says gems are heavily discounted 'government sale'. Items resold at home for a fraction. Do not buy gems from street strangers.

reports.title: Taxi refusing meter on Sukhumvit
reports.desc: Drivers near tourist hotels quote flat rates 3× higher. Say 'meter, please' firmly — if refused, exit and hail another taxi.

reports.title: Overpriced pad thai near Khao San Road
reports.desc: Street stalls here charge 5–10× standard Bangkok prices. Walk two streets away from Khao San for regular rates.

reports.title: Ping-pong show touts on Khao San Road at night
reports.desc: Aggressive touts outside bars offer 'free shows'. Entry may be free, drinks are not — and leaving before paying a 'minimum' leads to intimidation.

reports.title: BTS Skytrain is safe, well-lit, cheap
reports.desc: The elevated rail network is the safest way to move around Bangkok at any hour. Carry a Rabbit card (refillable, cheaper than cash fares).

reports.title: Cyclo overcharging in Old Quarter
reports.desc: Cyclo drivers agree verbally to 20,000 VND but demand 200,000 at destination. Agree on price in writing and confirm if it's per person before boarding.

reports.title: Fake Ha Long Bay tour operators in Old Quarter
reports.desc: Street agents sell 'budget' Ha Long cruises that don't match the brochure — old boats, no life vests, different itinerary. Book directly with certified operators.

reports.title: Motorbike phone grab near Hoan Kiem Lake
reports.desc: Thieves on motorbikes snatch phones from pedestrians walking close to the road. Keep phones in a pocket, not in hand, especially on the lakeside promenade.

reports.title: Lookalike hotel names in Old Quarter
reports.desc: Many hotels copy names of popular rated properties. Confirm address and check-in details via booking app — do not rely on the hotel name alone.

reports.title: Grab app is reliable and safe in Hanoi
reports.desc: Grab motorbike and car options have licensed drivers, GPS tracking, and in-app payment. Always safer than hailing a random xe ôm.

reports.title: Fake taxi at Noi Bai Airport
reports.place: Noi Bai International Airport, arrivals exit
reports.desc: Unlicensed drivers approach inside or just outside arrivals offering cheap rides. Rigged meters charge 1,000,000+ VND for a trip that should cost 250,000–350,000 VND (~$10–14 USD). Some claim your hotel is 'closed' and divert you to a partner hotel for commission. Use Grab before leaving the terminal, or only take Mai Linh (green cab, 1055) or Thanh Nga branded taxis from official stands.

reports.title: Fake taxi driver ATM robbery at airport
reports.place: Noi Bai International Airport, exit roads
reports.desc: Criminals posing as taxi drivers with name placards have robbed foreign visitors by forcing them to withdraw cash from ATMs. Confirm driver identity and licence plate before entering any car. Always pre-arrange airport pickup through your hotel.

reports.title: Aggressive shoe shiner in Old Quarter
reports.place: Hanoi Old Quarter, near Hoan Kiem Lake
reports.desc: Shiner grabs your shoe mid-walk or while you browse. Normal price is 20,000 VND/pair (<$1). After uninvited 'shine' or 'repair', the bill escalates to 50,000–500,000 VND. They know you won't walk away barefoot. Keep moving when approached — stopping is the trap.

reports.title: Donut / street food overcharge
reports.desc: Vendor offers a 'free taste', then loads a bag and charges 20,000–30,000 VND per piece (real price: 2,000–3,000 VND). Rapid Vietnamese and big numbers confuse. One traveller paid 200,000 VND (~$8) for a small bag. Avoid buying from wandering donut ladies entirely.

reports.title: Fruit basket photo trap
reports.place: Old Quarter, near Hoan Kiem Lake
reports.desc: Vendor in conical hat invites a 'free photo' with bamboo baskets or fruit. After the shot, demands aggressive payment — sometimes grabbing money directly from your hand. Others start peeling coconuts or fruit for you mid-photo and insist you buy at inflated price. Decline all unsolicited photo ops. Fair coconut price: 10,000–30,000 VND.

reports.title: Cyclo price ambush
reports.desc: Cyclo (3-wheel bicycle taxi) quotes 50,000 VND but demands 500,000 VND at destination, claiming per-person or per-hour fee instead of per-ride. Stops short of the agreed destination to extort more. Always confirm: total price, person vs ride, and duration. Track route on Google Maps. Pay only after reaching agreed destination.

reports.title: Xe Om motorbike taxi 'add a zero' fare scam
reports.desc: Agree on 50,000 VND verbally. Driver claims at the end you said 500,000 VND (adding a zero). No written record, no witnesses. Demand the fare be written on paper before riding. Use Grab for all motorbike rides — fare is fixed and GPS-tracked.

reports.title: Currency shortchange at exchange desk
reports.place: Hotels and travel agencies near Hoan Kiem Lake
reports.desc: Traveller exchanged $200 USD. Agreed rate shown on calculator but 4,060,000 VND placed on table instead of 4,600,000 VND. When challenged, the agent denied. Use ATMs inside banks or go directly to official bank counters. Never exchange at small travel agents or doorway shops.

reports.title: VND banknote confusion trick (50k vs 500k)
reports.desc: Vietnamese Dong trades in large numbers — 1 USD ≈ 25,000 VND. A 500,000 VND note looks similar to 50,000 VND to fresh arrivals. Vendors and drivers exploit jet-lagged tourists by short-changing or 'misreading' notes. Separate denominations in your wallet before paying. Never hand over large notes casually.

reports.title: Fake hotel redirect by taxi driver
reports.place: Noi Bai Airport and Hanoi Station taxi stands
reports.desc: Driver claims your booked hotel is full, closed, or flooded — and takes you to a commission-paying alternative. Some are copycat names of famous hotels. Insist on being taken to your hotel regardless. Have the address written in Vietnamese. Verify directly with the hotel before believing any taxi-driver claim.

reports.title: Friendly stranger card gambling trap
reports.place: Hoan Kiem Lake area and residential streets
reports.desc: Friendly local strikes up conversation near the lake, invites you home to 'meet a relative interested in your country'. While waiting, suggests a casual card game with a small initial wager. Stakes escalate rapidly. Some tourists have lost thousands of dollars in one evening. Never accept home invitations from new acquaintances.

reports.title: Fake charity 'survey' inside Thang Long Citadel
reports.desc: Well-dressed man hands tourists a 'survey form' for helping children. The form contains a signature line beneath a clause authorising a financial donation. Ignore any survey forms or unsolicited paperwork handed to you inside attractions. Real charities don't operate this way inside ticketed heritage sites.

reports.title: Stranded-student money request at Hoan Kiem Lake
reports.desc: Young Vietnamese man approaches, practises English, then hands over a pre-written note claiming to be stranded with wallet and phone stolen, needing money to get home. The note is already written in English — clearly pre-prepared. Politely decline and move on.

reports.title: Pre-expired SIM card sold at airport kiosk
reports.place: Noi Bai Airport and street-corner shops
reports.desc: Unofficial Viettel/Vinaphone sellers buy SIMs in bulk and resell them with validity already partially consumed. A '30-day unlimited' plan stops working after 6–10 days. Buy SIMs only from official carrier counters inside the airport terminal. The Viettel official booth is clearly marked.

reports.title: Fake Vietnam eVisa website
reports.desc: Third-party websites mirror the official Vietnam eVisa portal, appear as top Google results, and overcharge with 'VIP service', 'processing' or 'stamping' fees. Some harvest passport and credit card data without issuing a valid visa. Official site: evisa.gov.vn — only the .gov.vn domain is legitimate. Official fees: $25 (30-day single), $50 (90-day).

reports.title: Pre-damaged motorbike rental trap
reports.place: Rental shops in Old Quarter and backpacker areas
reports.desc: Shop issues a bike with pre-existing scratches or damage and withholds your passport as deposit. On return, claims you caused the damage and demands $200–500 USD in 'repairs'. Police involvement is threatened. Never surrender your original passport — offer a colour photocopy only. Photograph the entire bike before riding. Avoid shops requiring passport as collateral.

reports.title: Fake or clone tour operator names
reports.desc: Unlicensed agencies copy names of reputable operators (e.g. 'Mekong Tour' vs legitimate 'Mekong Tours'). They sell substandard or non-existent tours at inflated prices. Book only through operators with verified Tripadvisor listings and Google reviews. Confirm business registration if booking in person. Avoid agents who approach you on the street.

reports.title: 'Attraction is closed' redirect to commission shop
reports.place: Near Temple of Literature, Ho Chi Minh Mausoleum
reports.desc: Well-dressed local claims a famous landmark is closed for a private Vietnamese holiday or ceremony. Offers to take you to a 'more authentic' local spot — actually a high-commission lacquerware or gem shop with heavy buying pressure. Ignore anyone who claims an attraction is closed. Check Google Maps / official site directly.

reports.title: Fake photography student 'prints' scam
reports.place: Near Hoan Kiem Lake and major attractions
reports.desc: Person with a professional camera claims to be a university photography student looking for tourist subjects. Offers 'free' photos. After the shoot, insists on payment for printed copies. Legitimate students carry verifiable university IDs. Decline all unsolicited photography offers.

reports.title: 'Complimentary' snacks added to your bill
reports.place: Restaurants and street stalls, Old Quarter
reports.desc: Restaurants place bowls of peanuts, crackers, or spring rolls on your table without you ordering them. They appear free but are added to your bill — often at tourist-inflated prices. Wave away or return any unordered items immediately. Always request an itemised bill and cross-check.

reports.title: Fake monk blessing outside temples
reports.place: Ngoc Son Temple (Hoan Kiem) and Tran Quoc Pagoda
reports.desc: Person in monk robes approaches tourists outside temples, offers a blessing or ritual charm bracelet, places it on your wrist without permission. Then demands a large 'donation' — when you pay, they claim it's not enough for the 'full blessing'. Real monks don't solicit donations outside temple gates.

reports.title: Motorbike bag snatch
reports.place: Old Quarter alleys, Hoan Kiem Lake area
reports.desc: Thieves on motorbikes snatch bags, phones, and cameras from pedestrians — sometimes causing injury if you resist. Targets are shoulder bags, dangling phone in hand, and anyone walking on the outer edge of the pavement. Walk on the inner side. Use a crossbody bag worn in front. Never use your phone while standing on a kerb.

reports.title: Pickpocketing at crowded markets
reports.place: Dong Xuan Market, night markets, Old Quarter
reports.desc: Organised pickpocket teams work in crowded market lanes — one distracts (bumps into you, asks for directions) while another lifts a wallet, phone, or camera. Risk increases around Tet and Western holidays. Use front zip pockets. Leave non-essentials in the hotel safe. Carry only the day's cash.

reports.title: Drink spiking and methanol poisoning risk
reports.place: Bars and nightlife — Ta Hien Beer Street
reports.desc: Travellers have been assaulted after drinks were spiked. Cheap cocktails and local spirits occasionally contain methanol (industrial alcohol) — a known SE Asia risk. Never accept drinks from strangers. Don't leave drinks unattended. Stick to sealed beer (Bia Hoi) or reputable bar brands. Methanol symptoms include blurred vision, nausea, confusion — seek emergency care immediately.

reports.title: Unsafe tap water citywide
reports.desc: Tap water throughout Hanoi isn't safe to drink — even locals boil or filter it first. Ice at street stalls may be made from unfiltered water. Drink only bottled water with sealed caps or verified filtered water. When street food comes with ice, ask if it's from filtered water.

reports.title: Train Street is permanently closed — locals still try to sell access
reports.place: Tran Phu / Le Duan railway corridor, Old Quarter
reports.desc: Hanoi's famous Train Street was banned from tourist access in 2019 and permanently closed in 2022. Police patrol the area. However, some locals still offer 'secret' or unofficial access for a fee — this is illegal and results in fines. Don't pay anyone claiming to get you in. Alternative: watch trains from Long Bien Bridge instead (free, legal).

reports.title: Taxi meter set to miles instead of km (1.6× faster)
reports.desc: Some unscrupulous metered taxis switch the unit from km to miles — meter runs roughly 1.6× faster than it should. Always confirm before moving that the meter is set to km. Know your route on Google Maps. Legitimate rate is ~10,000–12,000 VND per km for Mai Linh taxis.

reports.title: Bus baggage 'fee' — no such thing exists
reports.place: Hanoi bus station and sleeper-bus services
reports.desc: Bus staff tell foreign passengers there's a fee to load luggage into the undercarriage. There's no official baggage fee on any Vietnamese bus service. Workers pocket the cash. Walk past, go to your assigned seat, and ignore anyone requesting luggage fees.

reports.title: Fake or overpriced concert and attraction tickets
reports.place: Outside Hanoi Opera House and major concert venues
reports.desc: Touts sell fake or marked-up tickets outside venues, claiming cheaper seats are sold out and only expensive alternatives remain. Always buy from official venue box offices. If turned away, ask a local for help purchasing. Don't buy from anyone standing outside an attraction.

reports.title: Pedestrian crossing danger — motorbikes everywhere
reports.place: Citywide, especially major intersections near Old Quarter
reports.desc: Hanoi has one of the world's highest motorbike densities. Motorbikes ride on pavements, ignore red lights, and go against traffic. Don't assume any crossing is safe. Cross slowly and steadily — never run. Maintain a constant pace so riders can calculate around you. Hesitation is more dangerous than slow steady movement. Hold children's and elderly's hands at every crossing.

reports.title: Fake gold quality in Deira Gold Souq
reports.desc: Gold sold as 22k or 24k tested elsewhere at 14k. Reputable sellers show UAE hallmarks — insist on a receipt with karat and weight.

reports.title: Unlicensed taxis near Dubai Mall exit
reports.desc: Men in street clothes offer rides cheaper than taxis. Unlicensed, no meter, may demand cash far above quoted rate. Use the official taxi rank or Careem app.

reports.title: Timeshare pitch at JBR beach walk
reports.desc: Staff offer 'free hotel breakfast' or gift in exchange for attending a 90-minute timeshare presentation. Sessions run 4+ hours and use high-pressure tactics.

reports.title: Restaurant hidden service charges Downtown
reports.desc: 5% municipality tax + 10% service charge added to bill not shown on menu prices. Check totals before paying — overcharging beyond these rates is illegal.

reports.title: Dubai Metro is safe, AC, and punctual
reports.desc: The Red and Green Lines are spotlessly safe. Pink carriages at front are women-and-children only — use them for comfort. Off-peak is very quiet.

reports.title: Bar overcharging foreigners in Hongdae
reports.desc: Staff quote one price, charge double at the end citing 'table fee' or 'service'. Photograph menus and confirm total before ordering.

reports.title: Counterfeit K-beauty products near Myeongdong
reports.desc: Street vendors sell fake Laneige / Sulwhasoo / COSRX. Products can cause skin reactions. Buy from brand stores or authorised pharmacies (Olive Young).

reports.title: Pickpockets at Namdaemun Market
reports.desc: Crowded weekend market is prime pickpocket territory. Use a cross-body bag kept in front. Card skimming also reported at small restaurants — use cash or Samsung Pay.

reports.title: Bar harassment near Itaewon late night
reports.desc: Solo women followed from bars on the main strip after midnight. Walk in groups, use official taxis from designated stands, and avoid narrow alleys off the main road.

reports.title: Insadong is well-lit and tourist-safe at night
reports.desc: The arts district has good street lighting, CCTV coverage, and is popular with families at all hours. Good base for evening solo walks.

reports.title: Gold ring scam on Pont d'Iéna
reports.desc: Stranger 'finds' a gold ring, offers it to you, then asks for money. The ring is worthless brass. Ignore and keep walking.

reports.title: 'Deaf charity' petition near Sacré-Cœur
reports.desc: Person approaches with a clipboard claiming to collect signatures for a deaf charity, then demands a cash donation. Not a registered charity — politely decline.

reports.title: Pickpockets on Métro Line 1
reports.desc: The tourist corridor between Louvre and Champs-Élysées is the most pickpocketed stretch in Europe. Hold bag in front, use RER B from CDG rather than Line 1 from stations.

reports.title: Overpriced drinks at Champs-Élysées bars
reports.desc: Bars on the main strip charge €18–25 for a beer. Walk one or two streets off the avenue for standard Parisian café prices.

reports.title: Friendship bracelet touts at Sacré-Cœur steps
reports.desc: Men tie a bracelet on your wrist before you can refuse, then demand €20 to remove it. Keep hands in pockets on the steps — if it happens, a firm 'non' and walking away works.

reports.title: Vélib' bike sharing is safe and cheap city-wide
reports.desc: The shared bike system has 20,000 bikes. Day pass is €5, and dedicated lanes cover most of the city. Good alternative to crowded Métro during tourist season.

reports.title: Taxi drivers at Bhuntar Bus Stop demand 5x the fair rate
reports.desc: Arrived from Delhi bus and was quoted ₹1,800 for the 30 km ride to Kasol. Fair rate is ₹300–350 on the local HRTC bus (runs every hour) or ₹600–700 shared taxi. Walk 50 metres to the local bus bay instead.

reports.title: Unlicensed guides offering 'secret routes' to Kheerganga
reports.desc: Guys posing as certified trekking guides offer ₹2,500–₹4,000 per person for Kheerganga. The route has well-marked signs and no guide is needed. Confirmed guides from registered operators cost ₹500–₹800 pp and carry ID cards.

reports.title: Only one ATM in Kasol — frequently empty on weekends
reports.desc: The sole ATM runs dry almost every weekend and during festival season. Shopkeepers and hotels then add 5–10% 'cash handling fees' or push card machines with hidden DCC charges. Carry ample cash from Bhuntar or Kullu town.

reports.title: Riverside guesthouses triple rates on arrival without notice
reports.desc: Online booking showed ₹800/night but was asked to pay ₹2,400 'peak weekend rate' on check-in with no refund. Always get a written (screenshot) confirmation of rate before arriving. Hostels like Zostel and The Hosteller have fixed advance pricing.

reports.title: Israeli cafés serving separate tourist menus with inflated prices
reports.desc: Several cafés hand foreign-looking tourists a 'menu' where a plate of hummus is ₹450 and a falafel wrap ₹380. The local-priced version of the same items is ₹180–₹220. Always ask for the printed menu and confirm prices before ordering.

reports.title: Strangers offer charas / hash in open market — police checkposts active
reports.desc: Drug peddlers approach tourists openly on the market street and the Chalal walking trail. Possession is illegal under the NDPS Act and carries a minimum 6-month sentence. Kullu police run surprise checkposts on the Bhuntar–Manikaran road. Decline firmly.

reports.title: Tent operators at Kheerganga top demand ₹1,500 per person on arrival
reports.place: Kheerganga camping ground, top of trek
reports.desc: Tents are abundant. Standard rate for a shared tent is ₹300–₹500 per person including basic dinner and breakfast. Operators approach tired trekkers as they arrive and quote ₹1,200–₹1,500. Bargain firmly or walk 100 m ahead to the next campsite.

reports.title: Roadside 'tour operator' offices selling packages with no inclusions delivered
reports.place: Roadside tour offices near Kasol junction
reports.desc: Paid ₹4,500 for a 'guided Sar Pass day trek' with meals and guide. On the day, no guide showed up and calls went unanswered. Book only with operators listed on Himachal Tourism's portal or with Tripadvisor-verified reviews. Avoid walk-in roadside stalls.

reports.title: Taxi drivers at Bhuntar airport claim the bus is 'cancelled'
reports.desc: Drivers tell flight arrivals the HRTC bus to Kasol is 'not running today' — a lie. The Bhuntar–Manikaran HRTC bus runs multiple times daily from 6:00 AM to 6:25 PM at ~₹80/person. Ignore and walk to the bus stand 300 m from the airport gate.

reports.title: Unattended bags at riverside campsites stolen while trekkers swim
reports.place: Chalal village riverbank, across suspension bridge
reports.desc: Multiple reports of bags, phones and wallets left on rocks being taken while tourists wade in the Parvati. Valuables should be left locked at your accommodation. Use a dry bag or bring a trusted companion to watch belongings.

reports.title: Touts selling ₹200 'forest trekking permits' — no such permit exists
reports.place: Kasol market, near trek starting signs
reports.desc: People in semi-official-looking vests stop trekkers and demand ₹150–₹200 for a 'forest entry permit' for the Chalal/Kheerganga trail. No such permit is required. The only check-in is a free registration at the forest department post. Walk past them.

reports.title: Fake 'priests' demanding mandatory donation at Manikaran temple entrance
reports.desc: Men posing as temple officials or priests stop tourists before the main gurudwara claiming a ₹500–₹1,000 donation is required for entry. Manikaran Sahib has free entry for everyone — no donation is mandatory. Proceed directly to the main entrance.

reports.title: Unlicensed riverside food stalls causing stomach problems
reports.place: Riverside food stalls, Kasol market edge
reports.desc: Several travellers report severe stomach infections after eating from makeshift stalls by the river without running water. Stick to established cafés with visible kitchens. Altitude and contaminated water make gut infections hit harder and heal slower here.

reports.title: Unprepared trekkers caught in sudden rain — path becomes dangerous
reports.place: Kheerganga trail, mid-section near Rudra Nag
reports.desc: The trail becomes slippery and narrow sections near Rudranag waterfall turn hazardous in rain. Three tourists required rescue assistance in late 2024 monsoon. Check IMD Himachal weather before departing. Don't start the trek if rain is forecast. Carry a waterproof jacket regardless.

reports.title: Online bookings at non-existent 'guesthouses' taking advance deposits
reports.desc: At least three listings on informal booking sites showed fabricated photos and addresses. Guests paid ₹500–₹1,000 advance and arrived to find no such property. Book only via Zostel, The Hosteller, MakeMyTrip, or direct verified WhatsApp with a photo-ID check. Never pay full advance to unknown operators.

reports.title: Hemp clothing and Pahari shawl shops charging 4x market rate
reports.desc: A hemp jacket priced at ₹2,500 in Kasol market is available in Bhuntar for ₹600–₹800. Pahari wool shawls sold for ₹3,000 in Kasol tourist shops retail for ₹800 in Kullu town. For authentic Kullu shawls at government-fixed prices visit the HP Handicrafts Corporation outlet.

reports.title: Shared taxis to Tosh demand solo travellers pay for all 4 seats
reports.desc: Shared taxi fare to Tosh from Barshaini is ₹100–₹150 per person. Drivers quote ₹600–₹700 insisting no other passengers are coming. Wait 20–30 minutes at the stand and other trekkers will show up to share. Never pay the full vehicle rate unless urgent.

reports.title: No mobile network past Kasol — share your full plan before leaving
reports.place: Trail to Kheerganga, Tosh, and Malana
reports.desc: There is near-zero mobile signal beyond Kasol village. In 2024, multiple trekkers went missing or needed rescue because no one knew their itinerary. Always register your trek route at the forest department post, share your plan with your hotel/hostel, and carry a physical map.

reports.title: Touching walls or objects in Malana brings on-the-spot fines
reports.desc: Malana residents follow strict customs prohibiting outsiders from touching people, walls or temple objects. Violations result in mandatory on-the-spot purification rituals charged at ₹2,500–₹5,000 or forcible eviction. Photography near the temple is forbidden too. These rules are non-negotiable — read up before visiting.

reports.title: Phones snatched at night near main market and pub area
reports.desc: Rise in phone snatching reported in late evening near the market and the bridge. Incidents mostly involve motorcycles or quick-grab tactics in a crowd. Keep your phone in a front pocket or money belt after dark. Don't use your phone while walking at night in market lanes.

reports.title: Hot spring at Kheerganga can cause burns — no temperature signs
reports.place: Parvati Kund hot springs, Kheerganga top
reports.desc: Hot spring temperature varies sharply by entry point — some sections are scalding (>55°C). No temperature gauges or safety signs. In 2024 a tourist sustained burns from entering the source pool near the rocks. Use the main designated bathing area only and test with hand first.

reports.title: Cafés adding 'tourist service charge' not printed on menu
reports.desc: Bills arrive with a 15–20% 'service charge' that doesn't appear on the displayed menu. When questioned, staff say it's mandatory. It isn't — service charge is not regulated as compulsory in HP hill areas. Refuse the charge, or confirm before ordering that the menu prices are final.

reports.title: Kasol–Manikaran road frequently blocked by landslides after rain
reports.desc: During and after monsoon (Jul–Sep) the narrow road is regularly blocked for hours or days. Tourists get stranded with no refund from operators. Check HRTC alerts and HP PWD road status before travelling. Don't schedule tight connections (flights, trains) immediately after Kasol stays during monsoon.

reports.title: Men posing as plainclothes police asking to inspect bags
reports.place: Kasol market lane and near bridge
reports.desc: Tourists report being stopped by men claiming to be narcotics plainclothes officers demanding to check bags. Real HP Police always carry visible ID and don't conduct searches in dark alleys. If stopped, demand to see ID and insist on going to the nearest police station. Don't hand over bags or wallets.

reports.title: Frequent power cuts — guesthouses charge extra for generator use
reports.desc: Power cuts are near-daily in Kasol, sometimes 4–8 hours long. Several guesthouses charge ₹200–₹500 extra per night for 'generator hours' that wasn't disclosed at booking. Confirm in writing before check-in that your booking explicitly states what is and isn't included.

reports.title: Unlicensed rafting operators near Kasol with no safety equipment
reports.place: Parvati River, near Jari and Kasol stretch
reports.desc: Local operators offer rafting without certified guides or proper life jackets. HP Tourism mandates Grade certification for all commercial rafting. At least two capsizing incidents on the Parvati stretch in 2024. Use only Kullu-registered rafting operators on the safer Beas river near Kullu town.

reports.title: Laundry shops charging ₹80–₹120 per item with no price board
reports.desc: Drop-off laundry services have no listed rates and charge ₹80–₹120 per item (jacket, jeans) when the going rate is ₹20–₹40. Confirm rate per item in writing before dropping off. Several travellers report items going 'missing' too — always get an itemised receipt.

reports.title: First-timers underestimating altitude at Kheerganga (3,050 m)
reports.desc: Kheerganga sits at ~3,050 m. Several tourists have experienced acute mountain sickness (AMS) and there's no doctor or medical facility within 12 km. Symptoms: headache, nausea, confusion. If symptoms appear, descend immediately. Avoid overexertion on day one and hydrate. Acclimatise a night in Kasol (1,580 m) first.

reports.title: WhatsApp 'Kasol Weekend Trek' advance scam
reports.desc: Fake 'Kasol Weekend Trek' WhatsApp groups advertise ₹1,800/person all-inclusive packages. Collect UPI advances (₹500) from 20–30 people, then the organiser goes offline. UPI is irreversible if sent to a fraudulent ID. Use platforms that hold escrow or operators with physical offices.

reports.title: Solo trekkers walking back from Chalal after dark face harassment
reports.desc: The trail back from Chalal village crosses a wooden bridge with no lighting. Female and solo travellers report verbal harassment and being followed. Return from Chalal before sunset (by 5:30–6 PM year-round). Go in groups and use a torch. If returning late, take a local guide.

reports.title: Violent attack at Sanapur — women assaulted, tourist killed
reports.place: Sanapur village, near Tungabhadra Dam, North Hampi
reports.desc: On 6 March 2025, a group of five tourists stargazing at Sanapur was attacked by three local construction workers. Two women were sexually assaulted and an Indian male tourist was murdered. Israeli and other foreign tourists evacuated. Three suspects arrested. Avoid isolated spots around Sanapur at night; stay in groups and inform your guesthouse of plans.

reports.title: Tuk-tuk mafia at Hospet Junction — 10× fare quotes
reports.desc: Auto-rickshaw drivers mob arriving trains and quote ₹800–2,000 for the 13 km ride to Hampi. Fair rate is ₹200–300. Drivers reportedly threaten competing drivers offering fair prices. If overcharged: walk 20 min to Hospet Bus Stand and take the KSRTC bus to Hampi for ₹18.

reports.title: Auto-rickshaw lie: 'No bus to Hampi'
reports.desc: Rickshaw drivers routinely tell tourists with a straight face that there's no bus between Hospet and Hampi. False. KSRTC buses run every 20–30 minutes between Hospet Bus Stand and Hampi for ₹18. Drivers tell this lie to force you into a paid rickshaw.

reports.title: 15+ tourist spots have NO security guards or CCTV
reports.place: Sugriva Cave, Sita Seragu, Chakrathirtha, behind Elephant Stable
reports.desc: Activists confirmed to The New Indian Express (Mar 2025) that more than 15 popular Hampi spots have zero security guards. CCTV exists only near Virupaksha, Vijaya Vittala, and Hemakuta. Isolated spots are especially risky after 3 PM when crowds thin. Explore in groups; avoid these areas after 4 PM.

reports.title: Unregulated homestays — no verification of guests
reports.desc: Authorities confirmed to The Federal (Mar 2025) that many homestays in North Hampi operate without proper licensing and don't record guest details. This was a contributing factor in the March 2025 attack. Following the incident, government forced closures of many North Hampi guesthouses. Verify your accommodation has a KSTDC or government registration number.

reports.title: Drug push near heritage zones — illegal, risk of arrest
reports.place: Anjanadri Hill, Matanga Hill, Virupapura Gaddi, Anegundi bridge
reports.desc: Cannabis is openly traded near several Hampi viewpoints, often by children posing as booksellers. Police filed 12 drug-related FIRs in 2023 and 10 in 2022. The March 2025 attackers were suspected to be under the influence. Cannabis possession in India can mean 6 months to 10 years imprisonment. Decline firmly.

reports.title: Coracle overcharging after 6 PM — 10× rate for night crossing
reports.place: Tungabhadra River crossing, near Virupaksha Temple
reports.desc: The last official ferry/coracle from North Hampi (Virupapura Gaddi) to South Hampi runs until ~5:30 PM. After that, boatmen charge ₹150–200 per crossing instead of the daytime ₹20–50. If you miss the last boat, the road route around is 40 km. Plan to be back across the river before 5:30 PM.

reports.title: Unlicensed 'guides' demanding ₹1,500–3,500 for day tours
reports.place: Hampi Bazaar, near Virupaksha Temple entrance
reports.desc: Unofficial guides quote ₹1,500–3,500 for day tours. The Karnataka Tourism Department has an official guide office near the temple with fixed rates: ₹500 for 4 hours, ₹800 for 8. Unlicensed guides lack knowledge and often take you to commission shops. Hire only through the official tourist office.

reports.title: Full-day auto tour massively overcharged for foreigners
reports.place: Throughout Hampi, starting Hampi Bazaar / hotel
reports.desc: Auto-rickshaw drivers quote ₹1,500–2,500 for a full-day Hampi sightseeing tour. Fair rate is ₹600–800 (off-season can be 40% lower). Drivers target foreign tourists. Negotiate firmly before entering and agree in writing or via phone notes. A cycle (₹100–150/day) or scooter (₹300–350/day) from Hampi Bazaar is far cheaper.

reports.title: Rocky boulders — multiple accidents climbing without guide
reports.place: Matanga Hill, Hemakuta Hill, Anjaneya Hill
reports.desc: Hampi's boulder terrain looks manageable but ledges and slopes are genuinely dangerous. A Tripadvisor reviewer in 2025 reported a sloping ledge around Matanga Hill where one slip means a fatal fall. No safety rails in many sections. Climb only in daylight, wear grip shoes (not flip-flops), avoid exploring rocky areas off-path alone.

reports.title: Aggressive monkeys snatch bags, phones, and food
reports.place: Virupaksha Temple, Monkey Temple (Anjaneya Hill), Hampi Bazaar
reports.desc: Monkeys throughout Hampi are bold and known to snatch items from tourists' hands and bags — phones, food, sunglasses, water bottles. Multiple Tripadvisor reviews (2024–2025) confirm monkeys jumping onto people at Anjaneya Hill if food is visible. Keep all food sealed inside a backpack. If a monkey grabs something, do NOT fight back — they can bite.

reports.title: Vittala Temple ticket touts — only buy at the official booth
reports.desc: Touts near the Vittala Temple sell 'unofficial tickets' at inflated prices or claim the booth is closed. Official KSRTC electric vehicle from main entrance: ₹40 per person. Foreign tourist fee ₹600; Indian fee ₹40. Only buy from official ASI/KSTDC booths. Site opens 8:30 AM, closes 5:30 PM.

reports.title: Summer heat: 43°C in Apr–May with no shade
reports.place: Throughout Hampi ruins — Royal Enclosure, Elephant Stables area
reports.desc: Hampi in April–May regularly hits 43°C. Open granite ruins absorb heat and re-radiate it. Almost no shade structures between monuments. Multiple advisories warn against peak summer visits. If you go, carry minimum 2 L water + ORS, and limit outdoor activity to 7–10 AM and 4–6 PM.

reports.title: No chilled water during power cuts at monument kiosks
reports.place: Hampi monument-area kiosks, Hampi Bazaar shops
reports.desc: Power outages are common in Hampi, especially in peak season. When power cuts hit, shops can't chill water. Tourists exploring far ruins (Elephant Stables, Queen's Bath, Achyutaraya) in heat face serious dehydration risk. Always carry at least 1.5 L of water from your guesthouse before heading out in the morning.

reports.title: Tourist spots completely unsafe after dark — no lighting, no security
reports.desc: A Koppal police officer confirmed to The Federal (Mar 2025): 'Tourist spots become unsafe after dark due to poor lighting and no security.' Avoid solo walks after sunset, especially along the riverfront, between ruins, or any area outside Hampi Bazaar. Even in groups, stick to well-lit accommodation areas after 8 PM.

reports.title: Scooter overcharge at Hippie Island crossing — agreed price not honoured
reports.desc: Scooter rental operators near the Tungabhadra crossing in North Hampi sometimes quote one price (₹300/day) and demand ₹500–700 on return, claiming 'km overage' or 'fuel.' Agree on total price in writing before taking the scooter. Fair rate ₹300–350/24 hours including fuel. Photograph the scooter before riding.

reports.title: Hampi Utsav: hotel rates 2–3× higher, town overwhelmed
reports.place: All Hampi during November Utsav festival
reports.desc: During the annual Hampi Utsav (November, dates vary), the town swells from ~2,000 to 50,000 people. Hotels spike rates 200–300% with no regulation. Transport blocked, replaced by overcrowded buses. Non-Indian women reported being stopped 50+ times per day for photos. Avoid Hampi during the festival unless you specifically want it.

reports.title: Peak season rooms 50% pricier — book well ahead
reports.place: All accommodation in Hampi and Hospet
reports.desc: Between October and March (peak season), Hampi accommodation prices rise 25–50% and quality guesthouses fill weeks ahead. Many travellers arrive to find their casually-confirmed booking not honoured. Always book in writing via a platform (Booking.com, Hostelworld) and get confirmation. Don't rely on WhatsApp-only verbal agreements.

reports.title: Photography ban inside Virupaksha sanctum — phone seizure risk
reports.desc: Photography is strictly prohibited inside the main sanctum of Virupaksha Temple. Priests and security actively enforce this. Tourists have had phones temporarily seized and been required to delete photos. Respect the ban. Photography is allowed in the outer areas of the complex.

reports.title: Turned away from temples for showing skin — no spare cloth provided
reports.desc: Multiple active temples in Hampi require covered shoulders and knees. Unlike some Indian temples, Hampi temples generally don't provide loaner scarves. Tourists in shorts and tank tops have been refused entry. Carry a light shawl/sarong — also doubles as sun protection.

reports.title: Mango Tree Café overrated — long waits, average food
reports.place: Mango Tree Restaurant, near Virupaksha riverbank
reports.desc: Mango Tree is Hampi's most-hyped restaurant but 2024–2025 reviews regularly call it overrated. During peak season, 45–60 minute waits are common. Food quality inconsistent. Better value at small dhabas around Hampi Bazaar. If you do visit, go early (8 AM) or late (after 8 PM).

reports.title: ATMs scarce in Hampi village and Hippie Island — carry cash
reports.desc: Hampi village has very limited ATMs and they frequently run out or malfunction. North Hampi (Hippie Island) has virtually none. The nearest reliable ATMs are in Hospet (13 km away). Carry sufficient cash for your entire Hampi stay before arriving. ATM fee is ₹27.14 per non-home-bank withdrawal since May 2025.

reports.title: Bouldering gear unverified — crash pads in poor condition
reports.place: Rishimukh Plateau, North Hampi bouldering area
reports.desc: After the 2020 demolition of Hippie Island guesthouses, bouldering rental services are informal and gear is not safety-certified. Climbers report crash pads with torn covers and uneven foam. Hampi's granite slabs are world-class but the base infrastructure isn't. Inspect all gear before use; novices should hire only an experienced local guide.

reports.title: Mosquitoes at dusk — risk of malaria and dengue
reports.desc: North Hampi villages near the river are dense with mosquitoes after 5 PM. Hampi is in an active malaria and dengue zone. Apply repellent before sunset; sleep under a net if your guesthouse doesn't have window screens. Carry DEET-based repellent from home — local shops often only stock lower-efficacy citronella.

reports.title: Coracle rides without life jackets — ask before boarding
reports.place: Tungabhadra River, all coracle points (Virupaksha, Kodandarama, Vittala)
reports.desc: Coracle boatmen sometimes don't offer life jackets, especially for short crossings. Some guides spin the coracle rapidly, which can cause disorientation and falls. The Tungabhadra can be deep and fast-moving. Always ask for a life jacket before boarding. If the guide plans to spin and you don't want this, say so clearly upfront.

reports.title: No mobile signal in remote ruins — tell someone your plan
reports.place: Achyutaraya Temple, Queen's Bath, Zanana Enclosure
reports.desc: Mobile signal is completely absent in several sections of the Hampi ruins. If you get injured or lost in the boulder terrain, help can't reach you. Always tell your guesthouse or a companion which ruins you're exploring and your expected return time. Jio and Airtel SIMs get slightly better coverage. Download offline maps before going.

reports.title: Road accidents on Hampi's potholes — scooters after dark very risky
reports.place: Roads between Hampi Bazaar, Kamalapur, Anegundi
reports.desc: India has one of the world's highest traffic fatality rates. Hampi's roads combine fast vehicles, roadside boulders, livestock, and pedestrians.
